EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm brought heavy rain and snow, flash flooding and gusty winds to the area. Rainfall totals from this storm generally ranged between 2 and 6 inches with locally higher amounts in some foothill areas. With such rainfall amounts, there was significant snowfall totals in the local mountains with up to 28 inches of snow reported at the resort level. Additionally, the heavy rain did generate several flash flooding events including several mud and debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall over the Solimar burn scar resulted in a significant mud and debris flow. The southbound lanes of Highway 101 were closed due to water and debris across the roadway.

CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY MOUNTAINS EXCLUDING THE SANTA MONICA RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another powerful winter storm took aim upon Southwestern California, bringing heavy rain and snow, thunderstorms, and gusty winds. Rainfall totals ranged from 0.50 to 1.50 inches across coastal and valley areas to up to 4.50 inches in the mountains. With such high rainfall totals, some significant snowfall was reported in the mountains with resort levels reporting up to 18 inches. Some severe thunderstorms developed with strong thunderstorm winds (in excess of 60 MPH) reported. Additionally, strong winds were reported across many areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winter storm conditions developed across the mountains of Los Angeles county. Above 6000 feet, between 12 and 18 inches of snow was reported along with wind gusts between 65 and 75 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One in a series of fronts brought high winds to parts of northern California...mostly at higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KNF-98 RAWS recorded a gust to 62 mph at 12/1920 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold temperatures combined with moderate winds to create very low wind chill temperatures over parts of northeast California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Juniper Creek RAWS recorded wind chill temperatures ranging from -16F to -24F during this interval.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow, ice, and freezing rain fell across northern California on these days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Mount Shasta City reported ice accumulations of 0.5 inches in freezing rain.  He stated the the back roads and sidewalks in northern Mount Shasta City were an ice rink.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of cold fronts brought high winds to portions of northern California...mostly over higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KNF-98 RAWS recorded gusts exceeding 62 mph continuously during this interval.  The peak gust was 69 mph at 19/1120 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of cold fronts brought high winds to portions of northern California, mostly over higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KNF-98 RAWS recorded gusts exceeding 61 mph continuously from 21/2221 PST until 22/0120 PST.  The peak gust was 71 mph recorded at 22/0120 PST.  The Bolam RAWS recorded a gust to 59 mph at 22/0103 PST.  The Van Bremmer RAWS recorded a gust to 60 mph at 22/0131 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was a landslide in the Happy Camp Complex Burn Scar Area in the Walker Creek drainage. It occurred on or about 1/17/16 with about 10 tons of debris. Heavy equipment was required to remove the material and the road was closed for at least several days. Heavy sediment caused ponds to fill solidly with silt at homes in Seiad Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a landslide in the Happy Camp Complex Burn Scar Area in the Walker Creek drainage. It occurred on or about 1/17/16 with about 10 tons of debris. Heavy equipment was required to remove the material and the road was closed for at least several days. Heavy sediment caused ponds to fill solidly with silt at homes in Seiad Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front brought heavy snow with moderate to high snowfall rates from the evening of the 17th through the morning of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two avalanches hit Alpine Meadows Road the morning of the 18th, affecting a bus and two cars. The bus impacted a garage and caused damage, while the occupants of one car had to be dug out by fire and ski area officials. No injuries were noted due to the avalanches. Alpine Meadows Ski area was closed for the day due to high avalanche danger.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front brought heavy snow with moderate to high snowfall rates from the evening of the 17th through the morning of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 6 and 11.5 inches of snow fell between Eagle Lake and Susanville south to near Portola and Blairsden. At the higher elevations, between 11 and 16 inches (estimated) fell at the Grizzly Ridge HADS and Independence Creek SNOTEL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front brought heavy snow with moderate to high snowfall rates from the evening of the 17th through the morning of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve to 18 inches of snow fell near the crest and along highway 395 north of Bridgeport (near Devil's Gate Summit), with lesser amounts of 5 to 10 inches elsewhere.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A woman died in Death Valley of heat related causes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman died of heat related causes in Death Valley.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vehicle was stuck in water and debris on Highway 127 at mile marker 15.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet more monsoon moisture, this time aided by an approaching upper level low and jet streak, brought a mainly nocturnal outbreak of severe weather and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was substantial damage at the airport, including at least two airplanes. A nearby residence also had property and tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet more monsoon moisture, this time aided by an approaching upper level low and jet streak, brought a mainly nocturnal outbreak of severe weather and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A semi truck was stuck in flood waters on Highway 95, requiring a swift water rescue. The highway was closed from I-40 to NV Highway 163 until 1025 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system intensified on the afternoon of Friday, January 22, as it moved through Santa Cruz County. The front generated heavy rain in the Santa Cruz Mountains that afternoon and then further south across the Santa Lucia Mountains and Big Sur area of Monterey County late that afternoon through early evening. Rain rates in these areas reached maximum intensities of between 0.50 and 0.75 per hour.  These heavy rain rates occurred on saturated soils and triggered rock slides and debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rocks and mud flowed onto Highway 9 in Felton (near Felton Empire Road) early in the afternoon of January 22, 2016. The slide blocked the southbound lane of highway 9 and caused a traffic accident.  Three vehicles were involved in the accident which occurred at 12:48 pm. There were two injuries in the accident.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and debris were in the intersection of Goffs Road and National Trails Highway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 95 was impassable from Vidal Junction to mile marker 24 due to flooding and debris.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vehicle was stranded due to flash flooding on Highway 62.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility was reduced to zero near Panamint due to blowing dust and sand.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to five inches of mud covered Kelso Cima Road, requiring a grader to clear.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At 8:50 AM PST on the morning of January 6, 2016, a weak tornado touched down in a field between Highway 156 and Orchard Road approximately 7 miles north of Hollister, CA in San Benito County. Results from a damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service later that day showed the damage to be consistent with a tornado	of EF-0 intensity (maximum wind speeds of 75 MPH). The tornado lasted just a minute or two and caused spotty damage along a 3/4-mile length path 10-20 yards in width that crossed Orchard Road as it moved to the north-northeast. Two large barns/sheds were moderately-to-significantly damaged along with a fence and some trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 8:50 AM PST on the morning of January 6, 2016, a weak tornado touched down in a field between Highway 156 and Orchard Road approximately 7 miles north of Hollister, CA in San Benito County. Results from a damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service later that day showed the damage to be consistent with a tornado	of EF-0 intensity (maximum wind speeds of 75 MPH). The tornado lasted just a minute or two and caused spotty damage along a 3/4-mile length path 10-20 yards in width that crossed Orchard Road as it moved to the north-northeast. Two large barns/sheds were moderately-to-significantly damaged along with a fence and some trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of low pressure systems moving around a large longwave low centered over the Gulf of Alaska moved on shore from the west into central and northern California. These systems had tapped into moisture from the sub tropical jet and were characterized as atmospheric river type storms. High elevation heavy snows and low elevation rains along with winds accompanied these systems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yosemite National Park Service personnel measured 26.0 inches of snowfall for storm total.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, moist flow off the Pacific brought heavy snow to northeast California on the 12th and 13th followed by high winds for portions of Mono County on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 18 and 30 inches of snow fell fell near the Sierra crest and in the higher elevations south of Lake Tahoe. At lake level, periods of rain or rain mixed with snow cut down totals greatly with only 9 inches of snow in Tahoma and just under 6 inches at the South Lake Tahoe airport and in Tahoe City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air mass and cold front bring rain to the area resulting in minor flooding in Santa Cruz.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storms with a strong subtropical moisture plume brought widespread heavy rain, with mountain totals around 4 to 8 inches, 1 to 3 inches in the Valley. Some areas were impacted by flooding, particularly in the foothills and mountains. Snow was limited to pass levels and over higher peaks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were 2.5 of rain measured after midnight, 4 miles south of Oroville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively warm storm with a concentrated moisture plume brought heavy rain, especially in the mountains and foothills. Rain amounts from 5 to 8 inches were reported in the Sierra, and runoff caused minor flooding. Snow levels were high, with only the highest peaks seeing some wet snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Sims reported heavy rain, with 1.83 over 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A easterly moving low pressure system moved through central California bringing heavy rainfall to parts of the southern Sierra Nevada which was also enhanced at times by orographic lift.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding near Bagby Bridge on Highway 49.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A good fetch of tropical moisture surged into the area during the last weekend of January. Though, this was not the only impact during the later part of January as fog was also in play. In fact, the Thursday prior to the arrival of the moisture had enough of a break in activity for fog development. ||Then on Friday rain and snow showers began to fall, though snow levels where initially fairly high. The snow levels did fall throughout the event to nearly 3,000 feet above sea level. This first upper level system ejected out of the area early on Saturday morning, which resulted in severe wind gusts across Kern County.||Also on Saturday, due to a break in activity again the area experienced dense fog development. The showers resumed again on Sunday prior to the system ejecting out of the area. However, as the system ejected it increased the pressure gradient across Kern County, which resulted in severe wind gust into the evening Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lodgepole reported 25 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving system moved across northern California on the 14th and 15th bringing heavy snow to some lower valleys of northeast California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to 8 inches of snow fell in the lower valleys from between a few miles southwest of Susanville to Portola. Only 2 inches of snow fell in Susanville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continues in full force during the month of January, 2016. Fresno received 4.42 inch of rainfall while Bakersfield only received 1.95 of precipitation during the month. Even with these rainfall amount there has been little response in water storage across Central California. ||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The general trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures has continued this year. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Also, communities in Central California interior face state mandated reductions in municipal water use.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning on the January 3rd a series of upper level systems moved through the area. On the 3rd the resultant pressure gradient lead to some severe wind gust across Kern County. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Grapevine Peak RAWS reported a 82 MPH wind gust at 1132 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A good fetch of tropical moisture surged into the area during the last weekend of January. Though, this was not the only impact during the later part of January as fog was also in play. In fact, the Thursday prior to the arrival of the moisture had enough of a break in activity for fog development. ||Then on Friday rain and snow showers began to fall, though snow levels where initially fairly high. The snow levels did fall throughout the event to nearly 3,000 feet above sea level. This first upper level system ejected out of the area early on Saturday morning, which resulted in severe wind gusts across Kern County.||Also on Saturday, due to a break in activity again the area experienced dense fog development. The showers resumed again on Sunday prior to the system ejecting out of the area. However, as the system ejected it increased the pressure gradient across Kern County, which resulted in severe wind gust into the evening Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake Wishon reported 29 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest to north winds buffeted sections of Santa Barbara, Ventura and Los Angeles counties. Wind gusts up to 66 MPH were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Warm Springs RAWS sensor reported northwest wind gusts between 61 and 66 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure dropped from off the Oregon coast through northern California on the 31st, bringing heavy snow to portions of the Sierra and northeast California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to 7 inches of snow fell from Portola to Susanville, respectively, on the 31st with most of the snow falling before 0900PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure dropped from off the Oregon coast through northern California on the 31st, bringing heavy snow to portions of the Sierra and northeast California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 20 and 31 inches of snow fell near the Sierra crest, with about 12 inches at the Mammoth Ranger Station. Much less snow fell near highway 395 with between 5 and 6.5 inches near Lee Vining and only 1.5 inches in Bridgeport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continues in full force during the month of January, 2016. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. Also, communities in Central California interior face state mandated reductions in municipal water use.

In fact, the Thursday prior to the arrival of the moisture had enough of a break in activity for fog development. ||Then on Friday rain and snow showers began to fall, though snow levels where initially fairly high. The snow levels did fall throughout the event to nearly 3,000 feet above sea level. This first upper level system ejected out of the area early on Saturday morning, which resulted in severe wind gusts across Kern County.||Also on Saturday, due to a break in activity again the area experienced dense fog development. The showers resumed again on Sunday prior to the system ejecting out of the area. However, as the system ejected it increased the pressure gradient across Kern County, which resulted in severe wind gust into the evening Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS station in Inyokern reported a severe gust. At 0627, California Highway Patrol reported closer of Highway 14 at the intersection of 395 and 178.

CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY COASTS INCLUDING DOWNTOWN LOS ANGELES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High surf conditions developed at the Southern California beaches. One swimmer near Manhattan Beach was swept out to sea by the surf and died.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High surf conditions generated deadly rip currents near Manhattan Beach. One swimmer was swept out to sea and died.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough moved inland along the California Coast on the 31st. The trough was accompanied by a surface low that rapidly deepened in the Southern California Bight and an exceptionally strong Pacific Cold Front. A broken line of thunderstorms formed along the leading edge of the front, and combined with post frontal winds to produce extensive wind damage over the coast and valleys. The County of San Diego received over 500 calls for downed trees. While the wind were the main player with this system, significant precipitation was observed. A remnant atmospheric river produced efficient orographic effects, resulting in 2.5 to 4.5 inches of rain along the west and south-facing slopes. Snow levels fell as low as 4000 ft, with moderate accumulations above 6000 ft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple mesonet stations at Otay Mountain reported wind gusts in excess of 58 mph between 1614 and 2359 PST, with a peak gust of 74 mph. Nearby Lyons Peak gusted to 60 mph between 2135 and 2145 PST. Numerous large trees were downed in the surrounding valleys.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple mesonet stations measured wind gusts in excess of 58 mph all along the San Diego County coast. The strongest gust occurred at Cabrillo National Monument where a 64 mph gust was reported. The winds caused extensive tree damage along the coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The long term drought continued through January, though precipitation amounts for the month were much better than in recent years, about 150-200% of normal. This built an above normal snow pack for the northern Sierra and southern Cascades by the end of the month. Reservoirs across the area continued to increase but generally remained below normal levels. Folsom Lake was an exception to this, rising to 104% by the end of January after a record low late in the fall. The Department of Water Resources increased water delivery projections from 10 percent early in the month to 15 percent of full water allotments by the end of the month, due to the increased reservoir levels.||Extreme drought continued to be detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor across most the region, with exceptional drought for the northern San Joaquin Valley, the central and southern Sacramento Valley, the Burney Basin and the Sierra. ||Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect. He extended the executive order requiring Californians to continue conserving water through October 2016. At the end of January 2016, the California State Water Control Board approved an eight-month extension of the existing drought-related emergency regulations with just some minor adjustments.||Local Emergency Proclamations have been issued for El Dorado, Glenn, Lake, Plumas, San Joaquin, Shasta, Sutter, Tuolumne, and Yuba counties. The cities of Live Oak Lodi, and Ripon continue in a drought emergency. Drought task forces have been established in Butte, Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought. ||Mandatory water reductions of 25 percent as ordered by Governor Brown continued to be in effect statewide.||The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted by the drought, along with California Disaster Assistance Act money for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells. Nonprofit organizations in the San Joaquin Valley struggled to meet the needs of their clients for drought-related programs.||A study released in late December by the Carnegie Institution for Science counted as many as 58 million trees statewide suffering severe water loss marked by browning leaves and dying limbs that���s far greater than previously thought. An earlier study by the National Forest Service reported 29 million trees already dead. Repercussions of this are that wildfires are likely to become more destructive, rivers and lakes that are naturally cleaned by forest ecosystems may become degraded, and less carbon dioxide is apt to be soaked up by trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Household Water Supply Shortage Reporting System indicated there were still some homes without well water due to dry wells, with 2 in Shasta County, 34 at Tehama County, unchanged from December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over the northern Sacramento Valley, dropping large hail, up to 3 inches in diameter. There was also large amounts of small hail, up to 5 inches deep, bringing slippery road conditions, clogging storm drains and causing vehicle accidents. Multiple funnel clouds were reported, but no tornado touchdowns were observed. Heavy showers and thunderstorms also brought locally heavy rains into the Valley and foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large amounts of hail fell and accumulated, 4 to 5 inches deep on I5 and adjacent local roads. The hail was up to an inch in diameter. The hail brought very slippery conditions, causing 3 vehicles to slide into the ditch. These vehicles needed to be towed. The hail also caused long traffic delays. Snow plows were required to plow the hail off of I5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, low latitude jet stream brought a series of storms through Southern California with periods of moderate to heavy rain/snow, thunderstorms, gusty winds, and high surf. Three-day rainfall totals were around 2 to 7 inches for the coast/valley/foothill areas, and 1 to 3 inches for the deserts. The mountains received a wide range of snow amounts, ranging from 6 to 30 inches, most of which fell above 5000 feet. The highest amounts were in the San Bernardino and Riverside County Mountains, with 20 to 30 inches in several locations. Several trees, power lines and poles were downed from wind. A plane approaching San Diego International Airport was struck by lightning on the morning of the 7th, but no injuries or damage reported. Countless flooding reports also came out of the heavy rain accompanying this system, with southwestern San Diego County being hardest hit. Several reports of pea size hail came out of Carlsbad, Poway (accumulation of about 1 inch near I-15 and Carmel Mountain Road), and some in Orange County as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station approximately a mile WSW of the Carlsbad-Palomar Airport recorded two gusts to 62 mph as a line of storms passed through the area between 1450-1510 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough moved inland along the California Coast on the 31st. The trough was accompanied by a surface low that rapidly deepened in the Southern California Bight and an exceptionally strong Pacific Cold Front. A broken line of thunderstorms formed along the leading edge of the front, and combined with post frontal winds to produce extensive wind damage over the coast and valleys. The County of San Diego received over 500 calls for downed trees. While the wind were the main player with this system, significant precipitation was observed. A remnant atmospheric river produced efficient orographic effects, resulting in 2.5 to 4.5 inches of rain along the west and south-facing slopes. Snow levels fell as low as 4000 ft, with moderate accumulations above 6000 ft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 20 ft light tower on the North Mission Bay Jetty was destroyed by high winds. Winds at surrounding coastal mesonet sites were gusting to 60 mph. The tower was a steel lattice structure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet storm brought thunderstorms with heavy rain creating large puddles on roads and minor urban flooding, especially over portions of the northern Sacramento Valley and northeast foothills. Wind 40-50 mph brought down trees causing some power outages. There was 4-8 of snow above 6000 feet,  up to 10 inches of snow over the highest peaks. Snow brought a period of chain controls to I80 and Highway 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The main Lake Don Pedro Marina (LDPM) sustained damage to its structure that led to the evacuation of its employees until the weather system died down.  The facility was closed for repairs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, low latitude jet stream brought a series of storms through Southern California with periods of moderate to heavy rain/snow, thunderstorms, gusty winds, and high surf. Three-day rainfall totals were around 2 to 7 inches for the coast/valley/foothill areas, and 1 to 3 inches for the deserts. The mountains received a wide range of snow amounts, ranging from 6 to 30 inches, most of which fell above 5000 feet. The highest amounts were in the San Bernardino and Riverside County Mountains, with 20 to 30 inches in several locations. Several trees, power lines and poles were downed from wind. A plane approaching San Diego International Airport was struck by lightning on the morning of the 7th, but no injuries or damage reported. Countless flooding reports also came out of the heavy rain accompanying this system, with southwestern San Diego County being hardest hit. Several reports of pea size hail came out of Carlsbad, Poway (accumulation of about 1 inch near I-15 and Carmel Mountain Road), and some in Orange County as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 20-foot tree was knocked over by thunderstorm wind gusts estimated around 50 mph. The tree blocked Fortuna Ranch Road.

This was confirmed through correspondence with the Severe Prediction Center (SPC).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several storms fueled with moisture by an atmospheric river brought heavy rain and mountain snow, along with gusty winds. Some minor urban and small stream flooding occurred, mainly over the northern Sacramento Valley. Snow levels were initially high, above pass levels, then dropped to as low as 3000 feet by the end of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a 24 hour total of 4.11 inches of rain at Pacific House.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure dropped from off the Oregon coast through northern California on the 31st, bringing heavy snow to portions of the Sierra and northeast California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 80 was closed (time range estimated) due to a 25-car pileup near the California and Nevada state line caused by snow and ice on the freeway. The accident was on the eastbound side of the freeway; however, the westbound side of I-80 had to be closed to allow emergency vehicles to reach the scene and get injured persons to the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, low latitude jet stream brought a series of storms through Southern California with periods of moderate to heavy rain/snow, thunderstorms, gusty winds, and high surf. Three-day rainfall totals were around 2 to 7 inches for the coast/valley/foothill areas, and 1 to 3 inches for the deserts. The mountains received a wide range of snow amounts, ranging from 6 to 30 inches, most of which fell above 5000 feet. The highest amounts were in the San Bernardino and Riverside County Mountains, with 20 to 30 inches in several locations. Several trees, power lines and poles were downed from wind. A plane approaching San Diego International Airport was struck by lightning on the morning of the 7th, but no injuries or damage reported. Countless flooding reports also came out of the heavy rain accompanying this system, with southwestern San Diego County being hardest hit. Several reports of pea size hail came out of Carlsbad, Poway (accumulation of about 1 inch near I-15 and Carmel Mountain Road), and some in Orange County as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reported the 14th street off-ramp on Highway 91 in Riverside was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough moved inland along the California Coast on the 31st. The trough was accompanied by a surface low that rapidly deepened in the Southern California Bight and an exceptionally strong Pacific Cold Front. A broken line of thunderstorms formed along the leading edge of the front, and combined with post frontal winds to produce extensive wind damage over the coast and valleys. The County of San Diego received over 500 calls for downed trees. While the wind were the main player with this system, significant precipitation was observed. A remnant atmospheric river produced efficient orographic effects, resulting in 2.5 to 4.5 inches of rain along the west and south-facing slopes. Snow levels fell as low as 4000 ft, with moderate accumulations above 6000 ft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large rock slide blocking the south bound lanes of State Highway 18.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The long term drought continued through January, though precipitation amounts for the month were much better than in recent years, about 150-200% of normal. This built an above normal snow pack for the northern Sierra and southern Cascades by the end of the month. Reservoirs across the area continued to increase but generally remained below normal levels. Folsom Lake was an exception to this, rising to 104% by the end of January after a record low late in the fall. The Department of Water Resources increased water delivery projections from 10 percent early in the month to 15 percent of full water allotments by the end of the month, due to the increased reservoir levels.||Extreme drought continued to be detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor across most the region, with exceptional drought for the northern San Joaquin Valley, the central and southern Sacramento Valley, the Burney Basin and the Sierra. ||Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect. He extended the executive order requiring Californians to continue conserving water through October 2016. At the end of January 2016, the California State Water Control Board approved an eight-month extension of the existing drought-related emergency regulations with just some minor adjustments.||Local Emergency Proclamations have been issued for El Dorado, Glenn, Lake, Plumas, San Joaquin, Shasta, Sutter, Tuolumne, and Yuba counties. The cities of Live Oak Lodi, and Ripon continue in a drought emergency. Drought task forces have been established in Butte, Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought. ||Mandatory water reductions of 25 percent as ordered by Governor Brown continued to be in effect statewide.||The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted by the drought, along with California Disaster Assistance Act money for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells. Nonprofit organizations in the San Joaquin Valley struggled to meet the needs of their clients for drought-related programs.||A study released in late December by the Carnegie Institution for Science counted as many as 58 million trees statewide suffering severe water loss marked by browning leaves and dying limbs that���s far greater than previously thought. An earlier study by the National Forest Service reported 29 million trees already dead. Repercussions of this are that wildfires are likely to become more destructive, rivers and lakes that are naturally cleaned by forest ecosystems may become degraded, and less carbon dioxide is apt to be soaked up by trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Household Water Supply Shortage Reporting System had Calavereas county with 1 household (no change from Dec) and Tuolumne had 235 households (up 6 from December).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continues in full force during the month of January, 2016. Fresno received 4.42 inch of rainfall while Bakersfield only received 1.95 of precipitation during the month. Even with these rainfall amount there has been little response in water storage across Central California. ||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The general trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures has continued this year. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Also, communities in Central California interior face state mandated reductions in municipal water use.
One of the last of a series of fronts brought high winds to portions of northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bolam RAWS recorded a gust to 66 mph at 17/1503 PST, a gust to 64 mph at 17/1603 PST, and a gust to 58 mph at 17/1703 PST.  The Indian Well RAWS recorded a gust to 61 mph at 17/1501 PST and a gust to 60 mph at 17/1601 PST.  The Van Bremmer RAWS recorded a gust to 60 mph at 17/1531 PST.

A plane approaching San Diego International Airport was struck by lightning on the morning of the 7th, but no injuries or damage reported. Countless flooding reports also came out of the heavy rain accompanying this system, with southwestern San Diego County being hardest hit. Several reports of pea size hail came out of Carlsbad, Poway (accumulation of about 1 inch near I-15 and Carmel Mountain Road), and some in Orange County as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds damaged 8 buildings and downed power lines in Vista. A tornado is possible, but still under investigation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Orange County Public Works reported three moderate debris flows in the Silverado Canyon area between 1200-1400 PST on the 6th. Rainfall of 2.2 inches over past 6 hours, 1.65 inches over past 3 hours, and 0.71 inches over past hour were observed at Upper Silverado Canyon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The San Diego River at Fashion Valley crested above action stage 4 times, and above flood stage once between the afternoon of the 5th and the afternoon of the 7th. Flood stage was reached between 1930-2130 PST on the 5th, cresting at 11.9 feet around 2000 PST. This resulted in mostly minor flooding of surrounding areas in Mission Valley, including low water crossings and nearby parking lots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A good fetch of tropical moisture surged into the area during the last weekend of January. Though, this was not the only impact during the later part of January as fog was also in play. In fact, the Thursday prior to the arrival of the moisture had enough of a break in activity for fog development. ||Then on Friday rain and snow showers began to fall, though snow levels where initially fairly high. The snow levels did fall throughout the event to nearly 3,000 feet above sea level. This first upper level system ejected out of the area early on Saturday morning, which resulted in severe wind gusts across Kern County.||Also on Saturday, due to a break in activity again the area experienced dense fog development. The showers resumed again on Sunday prior to the system ejecting out of the area. However, as the system ejected it increased the pressure gradient across Kern County, which resulted in severe wind gust into the evening Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bird Springs Pass Raws experienced a severe gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continues in full force during the month of February, 2016. Fresno received 0.33 inch of rainfall while Bakersfield only received 0.18 inch of precipitation during the month. The low amounts of precipitation and above normal temperatures we received this month did allow some snow melt to occur. This did improve the water storage situation slightly, however soil moisture did decrease across the San Joaquin Valley due to the lack of rainfall.   ||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The general trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures has continued this year. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Also, communities in Central California interior face state mandated reductions in municipal water use.

A strong trough moved inland along the California Coast on the 31st. The trough was accompanied by a surface low that rapidly deepened in the Southern California Bight and an exceptionally strong Pacific Cold Front. A broken line of thunderstorms formed along the leading edge of the front, and combined with post frontal winds to produce extensive wind damage over the coast and valleys. The County of San Diego received over 500 calls for downed trees. While the wind were the main player with this system, significant precipitation was observed. A remnant atmospheric river produced efficient orographic effects, resulting in 2.5 to 4.5 inches of rain along the west and south-facing slopes. Snow levels fell as low as 4000 ft, with moderate accumulations above 6000 ft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds downed trees in Wrightwood and caused damage to multiple utility poles in the near Lake Arrowhead. The strongest measured winds were at the Heaps Peak mesonet station, where sustained winds in the upper 30s and a peak gust of 71 mph occurred from 1315 to 1515 PST.

Avalanche danger was rated as considerable by the Sierra Avalanche Center on the 14th when a skier went missing. Days of inclement weather around the 14th and high avalanche danger caused the search to be halted on the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A ski instructor exited into the backcountry from the Sugar Bowl ski area on the afternoon of the 14th. When he failed to return home that night, he was reported missing (on the 15th). Tracking and location information from his phone placed him  on the backside of Mount Judah in an area with recent avalanche activity...however, inclement weather forced the suspension of the search on the 19th. ||On February 29, search and rescue teams using the cell phone information and dogs located the skier under 5 feet of snow. The time of the avalanche was estimated by the Sierra Avalanche Center.

A broken line of thunderstorms formed along the leading edge of the front, and combined with post frontal winds to produce extensive wind damage over the coast and valleys. The County of San Diego received over 500 calls for downed trees. While the wind were the main player with this system, significant precipitation was observed. A remnant atmospheric river produced efficient orographic effects, resulting in 2.5 to 4.5 inches of rain along the west and south-facing slopes. Snow levels fell as low as 4000 ft, with moderate accumulations above 6000 ft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gust estimated at 60 mph by a trained spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over the northern Sacramento Valley, dropping large hail, up to 3 inches in diameter. There was also large amounts of small hail, up to 5 inches deep, bringing slippery road conditions, clogging storm drains and causing vehicle accidents. Multiple funnel clouds were reported, but no tornado touchdowns were observed. Heavy showers and thunderstorms also brought locally heavy rains into the Valley and foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding occurred on 10th street in Corning, with large amounts of 1/4 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continues in full force during the month of February, 2016. Fresno received 0.33 inch of rainfall while Bakersfield only received 0.18 inch of precipitation during the month. The low amounts of precipitation and above normal temperatures we received this month did allow some snow melt to occur. This did improve the water storage situation slightly, however soil moisture did decrease across the San Joaquin Valley due to the lack of rainfall.   ||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The general trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures has continued this year. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Also, communities in Central California interior face state mandated reductions in municipal water use.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought continued through the month of February. After a relatively wet January, a period of extremely dry and warm conditions returned for most of February. This prevented the snow pack for the northern Sierra and southern Cascades from growing much, and actually decreased it in some locations by the end of the month, down to around 90% of normal, 85% for the whole state. Reservoirs across the area continued to increase but generally remained below normal levels. Folsom Lake was an exception to this, rising to 111% by the end of February. The Department of Water Resources increased water delivery projections to 30% of requests, up from a 15% estimate in late January. However, the dry conditions through the month prevented a larger anticipated increase.||Extreme drought continued to be detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor across most the region, with exceptional drought for the northern San Joaquin Valley, the central and southern Sacramento Valley, the Burney Basin and the Sierra. A small portion of the northwest forecast area in the Coast Range was downgraded from extreme to severe drought by the end of the month.||Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect. He extended the executive order requiring Californians to continue conserving water through October 2016. At the end of January 2016, the California State Water Control Board approved an eight-month extension of the existing drought-related emergency regulations with just some minor adjustments. ||Local Emergency Proclamations have been issued for El Dorado, Glenn, Lake, Plumas, San Joaquin, Shasta, Sutter, Tuolumne, and Yuba counties. The cities of Live Oak Lodi, and Ripon continue in a drought emergency. Drought task forces have been established in Butte, Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought. ||Mandatory water reductions of 25 percent as ordered by Governor Brown continued to be in effect statewide, though Californians missed these targeted reductions for the month ||The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted by the drought, along with California Disaster Assistance Act money for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells. Nonprofit organizations in the San Joaquin Valley struggled to meet the needs of their clients for drought-related programs. ||A study released in late December by the Carnegie Institution for Science counted as many as 58 million trees statewide suffering severe water loss marked by browning leaves and dying limbs that���s far greater than previously thought. An earlier study by the National Forest Service reported 29 million trees already dead. Repercussions of this are that wildfires are likely to become more destructive, rivers and lakes that are naturally cleaned by forest ecosystems may become degraded, and less carbon dioxide is apt to be soaked up by trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Household Water Supply Shortage Reporting System indicated there were still some homes without well water due to dry wells, with 2 in Shasta County, 34 at Tehama County, unchanged from January.

An earlier study by the National Forest Service reported 29 million trees already dead. Repercussions of this are that wildfires are likely to become more destructive, rivers and lakes that are naturally cleaned by forest ecosystems may become degraded, and less carbon dioxide is apt to be soaked up by trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continues in full force during the month of February, 2016. Fresno received 0.33 inch of rainfall while Bakersfield only received 0.18 inch of precipitation during the month. The low amounts of precipitation and above normal temperatures we received this month did allow some snow melt to occur. This did improve the water storage situation slightly, however soil moisture did decrease across the San Joaquin Valley due to the lack of rainfall.   ||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The general trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures has continued this year. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Also, communities in Central California interior face state mandated reductions in municipal water use.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bakersfield Californian reported that Wonderful Almonds removed 10,000 acres of almonds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought continued through the month of March, but with significant improvements in mountain snow pack and most reservoir levels. After a period of extremely dry and warm conditions for most of February, a pattern of moist westerly flow brought a series of unusually wet storms in March. This added significantly to the snow pack for the northern Sierra and southern Cascades. Snow pack increased to around 97% of normal for those areas, while on average the whole state was 86%. Reservoirs across interior northern California continued to increase, with the two largest rising to above normal levels. Lake Shasta was 109% of normal by the end of the month, Lake Oroville was 114%. Folsom Lake was 110% of normal and had to make flood control releases. In contrast, Don Pedro and New Melones remained below normal. The Department of Water Resources increased water delivery projections to 45% of requests, up from a 30% estimate in late February. ||Exceptional to extreme drought continued to be detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor across most of the Central Valley. Much of Shasta and Lake counties improved from severe to moderate drought by the end of the month. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continues during the month of March, 2016. Fresno received 2.93 inch of rainfall while Bakersfield only received 0.45 inch of precipitation during the month. Increased precipitation and slightly above normal temperatures we received this month did allow for some snow melt to occur. This did improve the water storage situation slightly, however soil moisture did increase across the San Joaquin Valley north of Kern County due to the increase in rainfall. ||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The general trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures has continued this year. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Also, communities in Central California interior face state mandated reductions in municipal water use.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent drought conditions continued to be reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shallow marine layer brought dense fog to the coast of Orange and San Diego Counties on the 3rd and 4th of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Newport Beach lifeguards reported visibility as low as 1/8th of a mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure brought strong winds to the desert slopes of the Riverside County mountains, impacting the Coachella Music Festival during the evening of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager for the City of Indio reported strong winds at the Coachella Music Festival. The winds displaced numerous tents, causing damage to parked cars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Short duration strong localized downslope wind gusts on the leeward side of the Kern County Mountains most likely due to mountain wave action from strong orographically lifted mid to upper level westerly winds. Fairly stable conditions with nearby mid level high pressure were also present further creating mountain wave conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong gusty west to northwest winds of 19-32 mph sustained and a maximum wind gust of 80 mph were measured by the Indian Wells Canyon RAWS station between 2100 PST and 2230 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of low pressure systems moving around a large longwave low centered over the Gulf of Alaska moved on shore from the west into central and northern California. These systems had tapped into moisture from the sub tropical jet and were characterized as atmospheric river type storms. High elevation heavy snows and low elevation rains along with winds accompanied these systems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated report from 7NNW Devils Postpile SNOTEL of 22.0 inches of snowfall for storm total.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold frontal passage associated with low pressure system located over the Great Basin in southern Nevada moved southeastward through central California producing severe thunderstorms due to large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of 1.00 inch hail from thunderstorm 5 miles west-southwest of Oildale California in Kern County was received via Facebook.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of 1.25 inch hail from thunderstorm in Tulare California in Tulare County was received via Facebook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple troughs embedded within a broader upper-level low brought rain and snow showers, scattered thunderstorms with small hail, minor coastal flooding and gusty winds between the 6th and 8th. Precipitation totals ranged from 1.5 inches of rain in the coast and valleys to isolated 3 inch amounts in the mountains. Snow accumulated down to 3500 ft with up to 9 inches above 7000 ft. Considerable wind damage occurred with 2 lines of thunderstorms that moved through Orange and San Diego Counties on the 7th, with numerous trees down blocking roadways and damage to roofs, windows, and cars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Edom Hill mesonet reported a peak gust of 77 mph and sustained winds as high as 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitable water at or near all-time April records and an unseasonably strong, cold Pacific storm system came together to produce a very unusual April flash flooding episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water flowed across Highway 58.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought impacts continued through the month of April, but near seasonal values for Northern and Central Sierra mountain snow pack and the Big 3 northern reservoir levels meant some good news. The very active March resulted in much above average precipitation numbers which helped top off the reservoirs. In fact, they had to do some flood control releases on Folsom as it was above historical levels. Reservoirs across interior northern California continued to increase, with the three largest rising to above normal levels. Lake Shasta was 108% of normal by the end of the month, Lake Oroville was 118% and Folsom Lake was 113% of normal. In contrast, Don Pedro and New Melones remained below normal at 67% and 26% respectively. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water flowed over Old Woman Springs Road at San Juan Road. One vehicle was pushed off the road by the flood, resulting in one minor injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitable water at or near all-time April records and an unseasonably strong, cold Pacific storm system came together to produce a very unusual April flash flooding episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 62 was flooded southwest of Vidal Junction, with several vehicles backed up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitable water at or near all-time April records and an unseasonably strong, cold Pacific storm system came together to produce a very unusual April flash flooding episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding washed out Fort Irwin Road near mile marker 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were widespread strong northwest winds in the wake of the strong storm which helped produce the flash flooding several days earlier. Isolated high winds and damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were two gusts to 58 mph at Barstow-Daggett Airport (KDAG) and gusts to 58 and 59 mph at Bicycle Lake (KBYS) on Fort Irwin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple troughs embedded within a broader upper-level low brought rain and snow showers, scattered thunderstorms with small hail, minor coastal flooding and gusty winds between the 6th and 8th. Precipitation totals ranged from 1.5 inches of rain in the coast and valleys to isolated 3 inch amounts in the mountains. Snow accumulated down to 3500 ft with up to 9 inches above 7000 ft. Considerable wind damage occurred with 2 lines of thunderstorms that moved through Orange and San Diego Counties on the 7th, with numerous trees down blocking roadways and damage to roofs, windows, and cars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A resident in Irvine posted a youtube video of tree damage in Irvine at Kazan Street. Numerous very large tree branches down, some on top of cars. Branches look about a half foot in diameter with some branches about 20 feet in length. One tree branch broke an apartment window.

Considerable wind damage occurred with 2 lines of thunderstorms that moved through Orange and San Diego Counties on the 7th, with numerous trees down blocking roadways and damage to roofs, windows, and cars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike from a line of thunderstorms caused a roof fire at a house in Laguna Niguel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving, upper-level trough moved through southern California on the 11th, bringing mainly light to moderate rain and snow showers through early morning on the 12th. Rainfall amounts ranged from around one-tenth to one-half of an inch in the coast and valleys, up to one inch in the mountains, and around one-tenth of an inch in the deserts. Snow levels remained fairly high with this system, with 1 to 3 inches of snow falling above 6000 feet. Between 1400-1900 PST on the 11th, a line of convective showers (QLCS) with minimal lightning, but strong winds, moved through the area. Several locations reported 46-55 mph wind gusts and downed trees with the passage of the line, as well as a few much stronger gusts which downed power lines near Edom Hill and Riverside.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven power poles were snapped due to strong thunderstorm wind gusts at an intersection in Home Gardens near Corona according to police dept. Wind gusts of 51 mph were recorded at nearby Riverside Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought impacts continued through the month of April, but near seasonal values for Northern and Central Sierra mountain snow pack and the Big 3 northern reservoir levels meant some good news. The very active March resulted in much above average precipitation numbers which helped top off the reservoirs. In fact, they had to do some flood control releases on Folsom as it was above historical levels. Reservoirs across interior northern California continued to increase, with the three largest rising to above normal levels. Lake Shasta was 108% of normal by the end of the month, Lake Oroville was 118% and Folsom Lake was 113% of normal. In contrast, Don Pedro and New Melones remained below normal at 67% and 26% respectively. On April 21st, the Department of Water Resources increased water delivery projections to the State Water Project to 60%, up from a 45% estimate in late March. ||Severe to extreme drought continued to be detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor across most of the Central Valley; while Shasta and Lake counties remained in moderate drought by the end of the month. The remainder of the Central and northern portion of the Southern Sierra Nevada mountain areas remained in the severe to extreme drought.  In improvement from exceptional to extreme in the Sierra south of Highway 50. ||Groundwater aquifers recharged much more slowly than the surface reservoirs, with many in the Central Valley still falling toward record levels. ||Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect. He extended the executive order requiring Californians to continue conserving water through October 2016. At the end of January, 2016, the California State Water Control Board approved an eight-month extension of the existing drought-related emergency regulations with just some minor adjustments. ||Local Emergency Proclamations remain for Butte, Colusa, Calaveras, El Dorado, Glenn, Plumas, San Joaquin, Shasta, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tuolumne, and Yuba. The cities of  Live Oak (Sutter County), Lodi (San Joaquin County), Manteca (San Joaquin County), Portola (Plumas County), Ripon (San Joaquin County), and|West Sacramento (Yolo County). Drought task forces have been established in Butte, Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought. ||Mandatory water reductions of 25 percent as ordered by Governor Brown continued to be in effect statewide. ||The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted by the drought, along with California Disaster Assistance Act money for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells. Nonprofit organizations in the San Joaquin Valley struggled to meet the needs of their clients for drought-related programs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Household Water Supply Shortage Reporting System had Calavereas county with 1 household, unchanged from March, and Tuolumne county had 238 households, up 3 from March.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm over eastern Sacramento valley resulted in a gust front pushing out in front of the storm. This gust front resulted in 2 reported gustnado's in a rural area. No damage was reported, but wind speeds were estimated near 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two gustnados were sightednear the Interstate 5 exit at Maxwell. One crossed I-5 and quickly dispersed. The second one formed breifly off I 5 and was videotaped by a passing vehicle. A thunderstorm was west of the area, but there was no connection between the gustnado to the storm. No damage was  reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather system resulted in thunderstorms developing across the Southern Sacramento and Northern San Joaquin valley and adjacent foothills of the Sierra Nevada Mountains. The storms resulted in half inch hail deep enough to cause slick road conditions, and slight damage to a carport. Lightning struck a tree, causing a portion of it to split off. An EF0 tornado was also observed that uprooted a eucalyptus tree and moved a shed off it's foundation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half inch hail, 1 to 2 inches deep, covered area roads. This resulted in slick road conditions and did some minor damage to a mobile home carport, from the weight of the accumulated hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought continued through the month of March, but with significant improvements in mountain snow pack and most reservoir levels. After a period of extremely dry and warm conditions for most of February, a pattern of moist westerly flow brought a series of unusually wet storms in March. This added significantly to the snow pack for the northern Sierra and southern Cascades. Snow pack increased to around 97% of normal for those areas, while on average the whole state was 86%. Reservoirs across interior northern California continued to increase, with the two largest rising to above normal levels. Lake Shasta was 109% of normal by the end of the month, Lake Oroville was 114%. Folsom Lake was 110% of normal and had to make flood control releases. In contrast, Don Pedro and New Melones remained below normal. The Department of Water Resources increased water delivery projections to 45% of requests, up from a 30% estimate in late February. ||Exceptional to extreme drought continued to be detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor across most of the Central Valley. Much of Shasta and Lake counties improved from severe to moderate drought by the end of the month. Most other mountain areas improved from exceptional to severe drought, except the Sierra south of Highway 50.||Groundwater aquifers recharged much more slowly than the surface reservoirs, with many in the Central Valley still falling toward record levels.||Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect. He extended the executive order requiring Californians to continue conserving water through October 2016. At the end of January, 2016, the California State Water Control Board approved an eight-month extension of the existing drought-related emergency regulations with just some minor adjustments. ||Due to the wet month, DWR cancelled plans to install a drought barrier in the late spring to help control salinity in the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta. A drought barrier was erected in 2015 to prevent salt water from encroaching from the San Francisco Bay and remained from June to November.||Local Emergency Proclamations remain for El Dorado, Glenn, Lake, Plumas, San Joaquin, Shasta, Sutter, Tuolumne, and Yuba counties. The cities of Live Oak Lodi, and Ripon continue in a drought emergency. Drought task forces have been established in Butte, Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought. ||Mandatory water reductions of 25 percent as ordered by Governor Brown continued to be in effect statewide, though Californians missed these targeted reductions for the month. Residents and businesses only cut usage by 12 percent, which was the lowest monthly reduction since the mandate became effective in June. From April 2015 to February 2016, state regulators reported urban residents were able to cut back their water usage by 23.9 percent from June 2015 to February 2016, just about 1 percent short from Governor Jerry Brown's 25 percent goal.||The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted by the drought, along with California Disaster Assistance Act money for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells. Nonprofit organizations in the San Joaquin Valley struggled to meet the needs of their clients for drought-related programs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Household Water Supply Shortage Reporting System had 1 home without well water in Solano County, unchanged from February.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were 18.5 inches of snow measured by a spotter in Kingvale, 24 hour total.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continues during the month of March, 2016. Fresno received 2.93 inch of rainfall while Bakersfield only received 0.45 inch of precipitation during the month. Increased precipitation and slightly above normal temperatures we received this month did allow for some snow melt to occur. This did improve the water storage situation slightly, however soil moisture did increase across the San Joaquin Valley north of Kern County due to the increase in rainfall. ||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The general trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures has continued this year. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Also, communities in Central California interior face state mandated reductions in municipal water use.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent drought conditions continued to be reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm swept through the San Francisco Bay Area and Monterey Bay Area on Wednesday, January 6. This storm produced heavy rain, minor flooding, damaging winds, a funnel cloud, and one confirmed tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roadway flooded on Pleasant Hill Road at Mitchell Court. Roadway was completely flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific Northwest low pressure system moving southeast into the Great Basin area in southern Nevada region and the cold front associated with this low continued to move through south central California region. Strong upper southeasterly jet streak moving around the periphery of the aforementioned low and accompanying jet dynamics coupled with strong pressure gradients brought strong winds to the Kern County Desert areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received report of road closures due to high winds at west bound California state route 58 and California state highway 14 near exit 167 both near Mojave California from 23ABC news in Bakersfield.

CZ_NAME: NORTHEAST SISKIYOU AND NORTHWEST MODOC COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies allowed nighttime temperatures to drop to around freezing over the Klamath Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tulelake reported a low temperature of 32 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep moisture feed and cold front combined to bring heavy snow to the Sierra from late on the 5th through the morning of the 7th, mainly near and west of the crest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to 3 feet of snow fell near the crest above 7500 feet from the evening of the 5th through the morning of the 6th. Elsewhere, snowfall totals dropped off substantially, with 9 inches at the Mammoth Ranger Station, 3 to 6 inches in Lee Vining, and 1.5 inches in Bridgeport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep moisture feed and cold front combined to bring heavy snow to the Sierra from late on the 5th through the morning of the 7th, mainly near and west of the crest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of snow brought between 2 and 4.5 feet of snow above 6500 feet, mainly west of highway 89. Below 6500 feet and farther east, considerably less snow fell with around a foot in the Tahoma and Tahoe City areas, with only 6 inches for the South Lake Tahoe and Truckee airports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, moist flow off the Pacific brought heavy snow to northeast California on the 12th and 13th followed by high winds for portions of Mono County on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southwest winds sustained between 40 and 54 mph with gusts 60 to 81 mph were recorded at the Mammoth-Yosemite airport and the Valentine Eastern Sierra Reserve (both near highway 395), with the higher end of the wind range at the latter location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong winter storms moved across northern California Friday through Monday. Storms just a few days earlier had area small creeks and streams running high. Moisture from these storms resulted in numerous areas of low land flooding, small creek and stream flooding and main stem rivers getting high enough to flow over Weirs, that are part of the flood control system in the Northern Valley.  This made local news as it was the first time in 4 years that the Weirs flowed since CA drought began back in 2012.  There were also breezy to gusty winds with this system that resulted in power outages from downed trees and power lines.  Wet slushy snow over the Sierra Nevada passes resulted in numerous slow downs and delays from slide offs and other accidents. A CHP officer was fatally injured when he was struck by a vehicle that lost control on the slick roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported receiving 3.16 inches of rain in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure system moving southeastward through northern California was accompanied by a cold front that moved southeastward through central California which produced an onshore flow with increased pressure gradients producing breezy to windy conditions for the Kern County Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report received from Cache Creek APRS station of 34-41 mph sustained winds with peak wind gust of 59 mph at 0012 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure system moving southeastward through northern California was accompanied by a cold front that moved southeastward through central California which produced an onshore flow with increased pressure gradients producing breezy to windy conditions for the Kern County Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report received from Mojave NTPS station of peak wind gust of 64 mph at 0104 PST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report received from Mojave ASOS of sustained winds of 38-43 mph between 0040 PST and 0120 PST with a peak wind gust of 69 mph at 0120 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continues during the month of March, 2016. Fresno received 2.93 inch of rainfall while Bakersfield only received 0.45 inch of precipitation during the month. Increased precipitation and slightly above normal temperatures we received this month did allow for some snow melt to occur. This did improve the water storage situation slightly, however soil moisture did increase across the San Joaquin Valley north of Kern County due to the increase in rainfall. ||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The general trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures has continued this year. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Also, communities in Central California interior face state mandated reductions in municipal water use.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent drought conditions continued to be reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong winter storms moved across northern California Friday through Monday. Storms just a few days earlier had area small creeks and streams running high. Moisture from these storms resulted in numerous areas of low land flooding, small creek and stream flooding and main stem rivers getting high enough to flow over Weirs, that are part of the flood control system in the Northern Valley.  This made local news as it was the first time in 4 years that the Weirs flowed since CA drought began back in 2012.  There were also breezy to gusty winds with this system that resulted in power outages from downed trees and power lines.  Wet slushy snow over the Sierra Nevada passes resulted in numerous slow downs and delays from slide offs and other accidents. A CHP officer was fatally injured when he was struck by a vehicle that lost control on the slick roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported 1.01 inches of rain fell in 4 hours. Hasn't seen any flooding, but heard small creeks are running high and fast.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak (EF-0) tornado touched down in a field between Highway 156 and Orchard Road approximately 7 miles north of Hollister, CA in San Benito County. The tornado last just a minute or two and caused spotty damage along a 3/4-mile length path that was 10-20 yards in width. A large shed sustained significant damage with its roof blown off. The roof was also blown off a barn and there was significant damage to a fence on the same property.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of low pressure systems moving around a large longwave low centered over the Gulf of Alaska moved on shore from the west into central and northern California. These systems had tapped into moisture from the sub tropical jet and were characterized as atmospheric river type storms. High elevation heavy snows and low elevation rains along with winds accompanied these systems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated report from 7 SSE Florence Lake SNOTEL of 25.0 inches of snowfall for storm total.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Short duration strong localized downslope wind gusts on the leeward side of the Kern County Mountains most likely due to mountain wave action from strong orographically lifted mid to upper level westerly winds. Fairly stable conditions with nearby mid level high pressure were also present further creating mountain wave conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 2131 PST a report of a semi-truck blown over on northbound side of US Route 395 just south Neal Ranch Avenue north of Inyokern California was received. Three major injuries from the accident were also noted, one subject was knocked unconscious and had major bleeding from the head, a second subject had received a broken leg, and a third subject had major cuts on legs and arms.
