EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong winter storms moved across northern California Friday through Monday. Storms just a few days earlier had area small creeks and streams running high. Moisture from these storms resulted in numerous areas of low land flooding, small creek and stream flooding and main stem rivers getting high enough to flow over Weirs, that are part of the flood control system in the Northern Valley.  This made local news as it was the first time in 4 years that the Weirs flowed since CA drought began back in 2012.  There were also breezy to gusty winds with this system that resulted in power outages from downed trees and power lines.  Wet slushy snow over the Sierra Nevada passes resulted in numerous slow downs and delays from slide offs and other accidents. A CHP officer was fatally injured when he was struck by a vehicle that lost control on the slick roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kingvale shed reported a storm total of 36 inches of snow, receiving about 12 inches each day between early Saturday morning and Monday morning. They reported numerous spin outs and a few interstate holds as they cleared the snow and accidents through the event. On the evening of March 12th, a CHP officer was fatally injured when we he struck by a vehicle while outside his patrol vehicle directing traffic from an earlier vehicle accident. Excessive speed, an illegal lane change and slick roads were suspected reasons for the crash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought continued through the month of March, but with significant improvements in mountain snow pack and most reservoir levels. After a period of extremely dry and warm conditions for most of February, a pattern of moist westerly flow brought a series of unusually wet storms in March. This added significantly to the snow pack for the northern Sierra and southern Cascades. Snow pack increased to around 97% of normal for those areas, while on average the whole state was 86%. Reservoirs across interior northern California continued to increase, with the two largest rising to above normal levels. Lake Shasta was 109% of normal by the end of the month, Lake Oroville was 114%. Folsom Lake was 110% of normal and had to make flood control releases. In contrast, Don Pedro and New Melones remained below normal. The Department of Water Resources increased water delivery projections to 45% of requests, up from a 30% estimate in late February. ||Exceptional to extreme drought continued to be detected by the U.S. Drought Monitor across most of the Central Valley. Much of Shasta and Lake counties improved from severe to moderate drought by the end of the month. Most other mountain areas improved from exceptional to severe drought, except the Sierra south of Highway 50.||Groundwater aquifers recharged much more slowly than the surface reservoirs, with many in the Central Valley still falling toward record levels.||Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect. He extended the executive order requiring Californians to continue conserving water through October 2016. At the end of January, 2016, the California State Water Control Board approved an eight-month extension of the existing drought-related emergency regulations with just some minor adjustments. ||Due to the wet month, DWR cancelled plans to install a drought barrier in the late spring to help control salinity in the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta. A drought barrier was erected in 2015 to prevent salt water from encroaching from the San Francisco Bay and remained from June to November.||Local Emergency Proclamations remain for El Dorado, Glenn, Lake, Plumas, San Joaquin, Shasta, Sutter, Tuolumne, and Yuba counties. The cities of Live Oak Lodi, and Ripon continue in a drought emergency. Drought task forces have been established in Butte, Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought. ||Mandatory water reductions of 25 percent as ordered by Governor Brown continued to be in effect statewide, though Californians missed these targeted reductions for the month. Residents and businesses only cut usage by 12 percent, which was the lowest monthly reduction since the mandate became effective in June. From April 2015 to February 2016, state regulators reported urban residents were able to cut back their water usage by 23.9 percent from June 2015 to February 2016, just about 1 percent short from Governor Jerry Brown's 25 percent goal.||The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted by the drought, along with California Disaster Assistance Act money for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells. Nonprofit organizations in the San Joaquin Valley struggled to meet the needs of their clients for drought-related programs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Household Water Supply Shortage Reporting System indicated there were still some homes without well water due to dry wells, with 2 in Shasta County, 34 at Tehama County, unchanged from February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continues during the month of May, 2016. Fresno received 0.29 inches of rainfall while Bakersfield only received 0.55 inches of precipitation during the month. Increased precipitation earlier in the year and slightly below normal temperatures we received this month did allow for lower evaporation rates and soil moisture to maintain higher levels. Some snow melt did occur contributing to improved water storage levels. ||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The general trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures has continued this year. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Also, communities in Central California interior face state mandated reductions in municipal water use.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent drought conditions continued to be reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm swept through the San Francisco Bay Area and Monterey Bay Area on Wednesday, January 6. This storm produced heavy rain, minor flooding, damaging winds, a funnel cloud, and one confirmed tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US 101 Flooded at several on/off ramps. CHP reports water 1 to 3 feet deep, including at Silver Ave, South Van Ess, and I-80 at 7th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm swept through the San Francisco Bay Area and Monterey Bay Area on Wednesday, January 6. This storm produced heavy rain, minor flooding, damaging winds, a funnel cloud, and one confirmed tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Funnel cloud reported. Did not touch down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storms over the northern Pacific generated large long period swell which impacted the coast of Northern and Central California from January 16 through January 18. Swell increased through the day Saturday January 16 and then peaked Saturday Night and Sunday January 17. Swell height gradually decreased Sunday Night and into Monday January 18, but remained relatively high through the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five people were swept into the ocean by a 15-foot wave around 5 pm PST on Monday, January 18, 2016 at Bonny Doon Beach in Santa Cruz County. Three were able to climb out of the water and were later rescued by the U.S. Coast Guard while two remained missing and were presumed dead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system intensified on the afternoon of Friday, January 22, as it moved through Santa Cruz County. The front generated heavy rain in the Santa Cruz Mountains that afternoon and then further south across the Santa Lucia Mountains and Big Sur area of Monterey County late that afternoon through early evening. Rain rates in these areas reached maximum intensities of between 0.50 and 0.75 per hour.  These heavy rain rates occurred on saturated soils and triggered rock slides and debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on saturated soil triggered a rock slide/debris flow onto SR 1 near Captain Cooper Elementary School in Big Sur. Highway 1 was at least partially blocked.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved over northern California and brought a short period of heavy snow to the Lake Tahoe area on the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten to 18 inches of snow fell near the Sierra crest above 6500-7000 feet and in the higher elevations south of Lake Tahoe, with 5 to 10 inches below 6500 feet and around the shores of Lake Tahoe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought another round of mountain snow and valley rain. This system was a bit more focused on mountain locations along and north of Highway 50. There was some moderate rainfall amounts in the northern Sacramento Valley and northern Sierra foothills, but creeks and streams had time to recover between systems so no additional reports of flooding were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dodge Ridge ski resort reported 10 inches of snow from storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific Northwest low pressure system moving southeast into the Great Basin area in southern Nevada region and the cold front associated with this low continued to move through south central California region. Strong upper southeasterly jet streak moving around the periphery of the aforementioned low and accompanying jet dynamics coupled with strong pressure gradients brought strong winds to the Kern County Desert areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received report from Mojave ASOS between 1300 PST and 1430 PST of 45 to 50 mph sustained winds and peak wind gust of 61 mph at 1340 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A paddleboarder fell from his board into Lake Tahoe during rough waters and was lost.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rough water due to gusty winds between 20 and 35 mph caused a paddleboarder to fall from his board into Lake Tahoe while seeking help for his companions who had also fallen from their boards. CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY MOUNTAINS EXCLUDING THE SANTA MONICA RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet again, the combination of very dry vegetation and hot and dry weather contributed to a wildfire in the mountains of Los Angeles county. The San Gabriel Complex, consisting of the Fish and Reservoir fires, burned 5,399 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The San Gabriel Complex, which consisted of the Fish and Reservoir fires, scorched 5,399 acres in the mountains of Los Angeles county. The fire started in late June and continued into early July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Erskine Fire began on June 23, 2016 along Erskine Creek Road, 1 mile southwest of Lake Isabella in Kern County. The fire spread rapidly due to strong, gusty winds, hot temperatures and dry conditions on June 23 through 25. The growth of the fire slowed as winds decreased by June 26, however the fire continued to burn through early July before being contained on July 9. It burned 48,019 acres. The fire closed Highway 178 east of Bakersfield for several days with evacuation of numerous small communities in the Lake Isabella area. The fire destroyed 285 residences and 1 commercial building as well as damaging 12 other residences. There were 2 fatalities as an elderly couple were unable to escape the rapidly moving fire. The cost of containment as of July 9, was $23 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought impacts continued through the month of June, though the largest of the reservoirs in northern interior California were at or near normal levels due to a significant mountain snow pack melting. Very warm weather caused the snow to melt and run off earlier than expected, though.  The California Department of Water Resources revised its water supply estimate downward and predicted just 71% of the normal runoff during the months of heaviest snowmelt, reducing the water rivers and reservoirs usually receive. Lake Shasta was 107% of normal by the end of the month, Lake Oroville was 102%, Folsom Lake was 88%, and Don Pedro was 97%. New Melones continued to lag behind the other significant area reservoirs and was only 41% of normal. On April 21st, the Department of Water Resources increased water delivery projections to the State Water Project to 60%, up from the 45% estimate in late March. ||The U.S. Drought Monitor showed no change from May for the area, with severe drought across the southern Sacramento Valley; the northern and central Sacramento Valley and the northern Sierra in moderate drought, while Shasta County was abnormally dry. The remainder of the Central and northern portion of the Southern Sierra Nevada mountain areas remained in the severe to extreme drought. There remained extreme drought in the Sierra south of Highway 50. ||Groundwater aquifers recharged much more slowly than the surface reservoirs, with many in the Central Valley still falling toward record levels. Growers in the San Joaquin Valley continue to turn to groundwater with reduced surface water allocations. This heavy reliance on groundwater has resulted in declining water levels.||Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect. Governor Brown issued an executive order on May 9, 2016 that built on temporary statewide emergency water restrictions to establish longer-term water conservation measures, including permanent monthly water use reporting, new permanent water use standards in California communities and banned clearly wasteful practices such as hosing off sidewalks, driveways and other hardscapes. ||The local Emergency Proclamation for Butte County was removed in June. Local Emergency proclamations remain for Colusa, Calaveras, El Dorado, Glenn, Plumas, San Joaquin, Shasta, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tuolumne, and Yuba counties. The cities of Live Oak (Sutter County), Lodi (San Joaquin County), Manteca (San Joaquin County), Portola (Plumas County), Ripon (San Joaquin County), and West Sacramento (Yolo County). Drought task forces remain in Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought. ||Mandatory water reductions of 25% as ordered by Governor Brown were suspended in May due to the improved reservoir levels, with voluntary reductions requested. The State Water Board announced on June 6th that water conservation was strong in April with residents statewide reducing water use by 26.1% over the same month in 2013. In contrast, water conservation fell to 21.5% savings for the month of June 2016, down from 27.5% in June 2015. ||The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted by the drought, along with California Disaster Assistance Act money for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells. Nonprofit organizations in the San Joaquin Valley struggled to meet the needs of their clients for drought-related programs.||Tree mortality from drought and resulting bark beetle infestation continued to increase both in scale and intensity, especially in low elevation pine and oak woodlands along the Sierra Nevada Range. A US Forest Service- Forest Health Protection aerial survey showed tree mortality was especially high in the Stanislaus National Forest southward, though pockets of tree mortality were present as far north as eastern Shasta County. Tree mortality from the recent years of drought has been estimated at 66 million trees across California.||Drought conditions are blamed for an increase in blue-green algae across Northern California lakes. These algae can produce cyanotoxins, which in high enough concentrations can potentially cause allergic reactions, skin irritation, fever, diarrhea, vomiting, joint pain, liver damage and even cancer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Household Water Supply Shortage Reporting System indicated there were some homes without well water due to dry wells, with 228 in Stanislaus County and 10 in San Joaquin County, both unchanged since May. However, many communities in the Northern San Joaquin valley were still receiving food assistance as a direct result of the drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet storm system produced periods of heavy rain across the San Francisco Bay Area and north-central California coast on Friday, October 28, 2016. There were several reports of minor roadway flooding as well as Bay Area traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain causes a debris/rock flow along SR 128 (Sage Canyon Road) near Saint Helena. both lanes on SR 128 covered by debris and rocks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure cranked up temperatures over the region. Daily high temperatures were 15 to 20 degrees above average on the 19th and 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two hikers rescued from Bump and Grind Trail, after suffering from heat related illnesses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Border Fire started June 19th, in the middle of a intense heatwave. The fire burned several thousand acres and multiple structures before it was contained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Border Fire began from unknown causes on the 19th of June. A local couple was killed on their property by the fire. Five structures and eleven outbuildings were destroyed, with one other home suffering damage. The fire also destroyed numerous power and phone lines. Firefighters acquired 100% containment on the fire June 30th, after 7,609 acres had been burned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two low pressure systems brought a combination of strong winds and significant rain and high elevation snow on the 14th and 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blowing from west to east across June Lake caused very choppy waters on the 15th. Five males (none wearing life jackets) were in a boat which capsized, causing the death of three of the males. Two of the men reached shore and were treated and released. The three victims were found by divers on the morning of the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and dry atmosphere, gusty winds and very dry vegetation lead to two significant wildfires across Southern California. The Sherpa fire and Pine fire burned over 7,500 acres total across the mountains of Santa Barbara and Ventura counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sherpa Fire burned 7,474 acres in the mountains of Santa Barbara county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and dry atmosphere, gusty winds and very dry vegetation lead to two significant wildfires across Southern California. The Sherpa fire and Pine fire burned over 7,500 acres total across the mountains of Santa Barbara and Ventura counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pine Fire, which started in late June and burned into mid July, burned 2,304 acres in the mountains of Ventura county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought impacts continued through the month of June, though the largest of the reservoirs in northern interior California were at or near normal levels due to a significant mountain snow pack melting. Very warm weather caused the snow to melt and run off earlier than expected, though.  The California Department of Water Resources revised its water supply estimate downward and predicted just 71% of the normal runoff during the months of heaviest snowmelt, reducing the water rivers and reservoirs usually receive. Lake Shasta was 107% of normal by the end of the month, Lake Oroville was 102%, Folsom Lake was 88%, and Don Pedro was 97%. New Melones continued to lag behind the other significant area reservoirs and was only 41% of normal. On April 21st, the Department of Water Resources increased water delivery projections to the State Water Project to 60%, up from the 45% estimate in late March. ||The U.S. Drought Monitor showed no change from May for the area, with severe drought across the southern Sacramento Valley; the northern and central Sacramento Valley and the northern Sierra in moderate drought, while Shasta County was abnormally dry. The remainder of the Central and northern portion of the Southern Sierra Nevada mountain areas remained in the severe to extreme drought. There remained extreme drought in the Sierra south of Highway 50. ||Groundwater aquifers recharged much more slowly than the surface reservoirs, with many in the Central Valley still falling toward record levels. Growers in the San Joaquin Valley continue to turn to groundwater with reduced surface water allocations. This heavy reliance on groundwater has resulted in declining water levels.||Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect. Governor Brown issued an executive order on May 9, 2016 that built on temporary statewide emergency water restrictions to establish longer-term water conservation measures, including permanent monthly water use reporting, new permanent water use standards in California communities and banned clearly wasteful practices such as hosing off sidewalks, driveways and other hardscapes. ||The local Emergency Proclamation for Butte County was removed in June. Local Emergency proclamations remain for Colusa, Calaveras, El Dorado, Glenn, Plumas, San Joaquin, Shasta, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tuolumne, and Yuba counties. The cities of Live Oak (Sutter County), Lodi (San Joaquin County), Manteca (San Joaquin County), Portola (Plumas County), Ripon (San Joaquin County), and West Sacramento (Yolo County). Drought task forces remain in Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought. ||Mandatory water reductions of 25% as ordered by Governor Brown were suspended in May due to the improved reservoir levels, with voluntary reductions requested. The State Water Board announced on June 6th that water conservation was strong in April with residents statewide reducing water use by 26.1% over the same month in 2013. In contrast, water conservation fell to 21.5% savings for the month of June 2016, down from 27.5% in June 2015. ||The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted by the drought, along with California Disaster Assistance Act money for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells. Nonprofit organizations in the San Joaquin Valley struggled to meet the needs of their clients for drought-related programs.||Tree mortality from drought and resulting bark beetle infestation continued to increase both in scale and intensity, especially in low elevation pine and oak woodlands along the Sierra Nevada Range. A US Forest Service- Forest Health Protection aerial survey showed tree mortality was especially high in the Stanislaus National Forest southward, though pockets of tree mortality were present as far north as eastern Shasta County. Tree mortality from the recent years of drought has been estimated at 66 million trees across California.||Drought conditions are blamed for an increase in blue-green algae across Northern California lakes. These algae can produce cyanotoxins, which in high enough concentrations can potentially cause allergic reactions, skin irritation, fever, diarrhea, vomiting, joint pain, liver damage and even cancer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Household Water Supply Shortage Reporting System indicated there were still some homes without well water due to dry wells, with 3 in Shasta County, unchanged from May. Tehama County was 34, also unchanged from May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two low pressure systems brought a combination of strong winds and significant rain and high elevation snow on the 14th and 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds along Highway 395 between Susanville and Doyle caused the overturning of two semis between 0230PST and 0300PST. Power lines were downed due to fallen tree limbs in Susanville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of heat and low humidity increased wildfire danger at the end of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Trailhead Fire started on June 28th and continued into early July. The cause is still under investigation. There were a total of 5646 acres burned, with a total of 3 firefighters injured. The were 2600 structures threatened, with extensive evacuations in the area around Todd Valley and Forest Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet storm system produced periods of heavy rain across the San Francisco Bay Area and north-central California coast on Friday, October 28, 2016. There were several reports of minor roadway flooding as well as Bay Area traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain causes flooding on US Highway 101 near Soledad.  North Bound lane #2 closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous vehicle accidents were reported during 27/28 October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person was reported possibly trapped inside a car on northbound Highway 280 south of the 87 connector near the Reed Street offramp before 6 a.m. 10/27, according to the CHP and San Jose firefighters. ||Http://www.nbcbayarea.com/news/local/Slow-Down-Accidents-Abound-in-Bay-Area-as-Rain-Pelts-Highways-399016231.html.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet storm system produced periods of heavy rain across the San Francisco Bay Area and north-central California coast on Friday, October 28, 2016. There were several reports of minor roadway flooding as well as Bay Area traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Even as the sun began to break through as the rain eased up, drivers discovered flooded roads. One full lane of Senter Road across from San Jose's Kelley Park was impassable. Despite a warning sign, some big rigs and smaller vehicles plowed through the small lake anyway.||Http://abc7news.com/weather/heavy-rain-drenches-south-bay-/1578706/.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from the Gulf of Alaska into the West between the 22nd and 24th, bringing heavy snow to the higher elevations of the Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mammoth Mountain Ski area reported between 20 and 32 inches of snowfall on the 23rd, with 14 inches in Mammoth Lakes at 8200 ft. Eight to 15 inches is estimated to have fallen for Sierra crest and Sweetwater Range SNOTEL sites. For locations along Highway 395 between Bridgeport and Crowley Lake, 2.5 to 5.5 inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One person suffered heat stroke in Death Valley on June 1st (she survived), and another person was found dead, presumably from the heat, on June 9th. High temperatures at Furnace Creek during this period ranged from 115F to 120F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two people were stranded in the park when their car became stuck in loose sand. They walked for two miles before being found. One of them, described as an older woman, suffered from heat stroke. She recovered after a week in the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One person suffered heat stroke in Death Valley on June 1st (she survived), and another person was found dead, presumably from the heat, on June 9th. High temperatures at Furnace Creek during this period ranged from 115F to 120F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two park visitors found a dead motorcyclist on Harry Wade Road in the extreme southern end of the park June 9th. It is unknown when the man died.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning from isolated thunderstorms in the Owens Valley started a fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning started a fire near the gun club just north of West Bishop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet storm system produced periods of heavy rain across the San Francisco Bay Area and north-central California coast on Friday, October 28, 2016. There were several reports of minor roadway flooding as well as Bay Area traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain causes roadway flooding on Junction Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet storm system produced periods of heavy rain across the San Francisco Bay Area and north-central California coast on Friday, October 28, 2016. There were several reports of minor roadway flooding as well as Bay Area traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain causes roadway flooding near Las Lomas on Hall Road, between Pini and Johnson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first monsoon moisture push of the season led to isolated thunderstorms on the 27th through the 29th, followed by a widespread severe weather and flash flooding outbreak on the 30th. The episode continued into July 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A debris flow covered Highway 168 with mud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first monsoon moisture push of the season led to isolated thunderstorms on the 27th through the 29th, followed by a widespread severe weather and flash flooding outbreak on the 30th. The episode continued into July 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and mud covered the intersection of Highway 95 and Goffs Road. 95 was eventually closed all the way to Interstate 40.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first monsoon moisture push of the season led to isolated thunderstorms on the 27th through the 29th, followed by a widespread severe weather and flash flooding outbreak on the 30th. The episode continued into July 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 190 was flooded from Daylight Pass Cutoff to Cow Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first monsoon moisture push of the season led to isolated thunderstorms on the 27th through the 29th, followed by a widespread severe weather and flash flooding outbreak on the 30th. The episode continued into July 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding closed Needles Highway and River Road, and also washed out several nearby roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Gap Wildfire started on 08/27/16 at around 1945 PDT. The cause was unknown, but likely human caused. As of 0430 PDT on 09/18/16, the fire covered 33867 acres and was 100% contained. 14 structures were lost and 29.7 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gap Wildfire started on 08/27/16 at around 1945 PDT. The cause was unknown, but likely human caused. As of 0430 PDT on 09/18/16, the fire covered 33867 acres and was 100% contained. 14 structures were lost and 29.7 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several pushes of southerly swell produced periods of high surf along the Orange County beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lifeguards in Huntington Beach and Newport Beach reported sets to 8 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several pushes of southerly swell produced periods of high surf along the Orange County beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lifeguards in Huntington Beach and Newport Beach reported surf heights to 8 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough off the SoCal coast pulled tropical moisture from former Hurricane Paine northward into the area, bringing unsettled weather from the 19th-21st. Unusual lightning activity occurred over the San Diego Metro on the 19th. San Diego and Riverside Counties saw unseasonable rainfall, with one-quarter to one-half of an inch falling over the coastal and valleys, and one-half to one and a half inches in the mountains and deserts. The rainfall delayed a San Diego Padres baseball game at Petco Park on the 20th, and increased September rainfall to more than 200% of normal over San Diego and Riverside Counties. When the trough moved through the region on the 22nd strong winds surfaced in the deserts bring areas of blowing dust and sand.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of convection formed along the leading edge of moisture from former Hurricane Paine as it moved northward. Numerous lightning strikes occurred in San Diego County setting palm trees on fire and causing power outages to 4000 customers.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds produced blowing dust and sand with visibility of 1/2 t 3/4 of a mile, impacting travel on highway 78 near Ocotillo Wells.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving upper level low pressure system just west of the coast of California allowed for a large influx of moisture to move into central California which set the stage for heavy rain bands and embedded thunderstorms to develop for two days in a row. Flooding was observed in many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported water covering northbound SR99 at the Belmont off-ramp in Fresno at 1710 PST.

Flooding was observed in many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported road flooding on SR152 at Rock Bridge west of Los Banos at 1822 PST.

Flooding was observed in many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported road closure due to road flooding south of Panoche Junction on Kamm Road just east of Interstate 5 to San Diego Road at 2259 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level trough and an associated cold front moved south and east into the central California interior and brought gusty winds to the Kern County desert areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report received from Mojave ASOS of sustained winds of 43-50 mph between 1600 PST and 1700 PST with a peak wind gust of 60 mph at 1620 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level ridge and offshore flow brought very hot temperatures and low relative humidity to areas west of the mountains. Temperatures ranged from 85 along the coast to 105 degrees in the San Diego County valleys and Inland Empire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A survey of 19 local hospitals indicated 20 heat related 911 calls over two days, with 3 heat related injuries and 1 death. These numbers do not include urgent care facilities and are therefore likely underdone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low off the SoCal coast en-trained moisture from northern Mexico, resulting showers and isolated thunderstorms over the mountains and deserts on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported minor urban flooding from a nearly stationary thunderstorm in Pine Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continues during the month of September, 2016. Both Fresno and Bakersfield received no precipitation during the month. No precipitation and near normal temperatures we received this month did allow for increased evaporation rates and soil moisture to decrease. ||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The general trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures has continued this year. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Also, communities in Central California interior face state mandated reductions in municipal water use.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent drought conditions continued to be reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Cedar fire began on 8/16/16 5 miles north of Alta Sierra in Kern County and spread northward into Tulare County. It was human caused. The fire burned 29,322 acres mostly during August 2016, before being fully contained on 9/30/16. The fire forced evacuations for several communities in Kern and Tulare counties for more than two weeks and Highway 155 was closed for several days due to the fire. Six cabins in the Spear Creek Summer Homes tract in the community of Posey in Tulare County were destroyed. The cost of containment was $58.4 million.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chimney Fire, in northern San Luis Obispo county, was finally contained in early September. In total, the fire burned 46,344 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought impacts continued through the month of May, though the largest of the reservoirs in northern interior California were at or above normal levels due to a significant mountain snow pack melting. Lake Shasta was 107% of normal by the end of the month, Lake Oroville was 111%, Folsom Lake was 101%, and Don Pedro was 99%. New Melones continued to lag behind the other significant area reservoirs and was only 41% of normal. On April 21st, the Department of Water Resources increased water delivery projections to the State Water Project to 60%, up from the 45% estimate in late March. ||The U.S. Drought Monitor showed improvement from extreme to severe drought across the southern Sacramento Valley; the northern and central Sacramento Valley and the northern Sierra improved from severe to moderate drought, while Shasta County improved from moderate drought to abnormally dry by the end of the month. The remainder of the Central and northern portion of the Southern Sierra Nevada mountain areas remained in the severe to extreme drought. There was improvement from exceptional to extreme in the Sierra south of Highway 50. ||April-July forecasts remained the same or dropped when compared to the April 1 forecast.  The changes were -5, and 0 respectively in the Sacramento and San Joaquin regions.  Despite the presence last Fall of warmer-than-normal tropical sea surface temperatures and the thought by some that they would contribute to a very wet California winter, the April-July forecasts for the three main Sierra regions were below average.   The water year forecasts are 102 and 91 percent of average for the Sacramento and San Joaquin regions.||Groundwater aquifers recharged much more slowly than the surface reservoirs, with many in the Central Valley still falling toward record levels. ||Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect. Governor Brown issued an executive order on May 9, 2016 that built on temporary statewide emergency water restrictions to establish longer-term water conservation measures, including permanent monthly water use reporting, new permanent water use standards in California communities and banned clearly wasteful practices such as hosing off sidewalks, driveways and other hardscapes.||Local Emergency Proclamations remain for Butte, Colusa, Calaveras, El Dorado, Glenn, Plumas, San Joaquin, Shasta, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tuolumne, and Yuba. The cities of Live Oak (Sutter County), Lodi (San Joaquin County), Manteca (San Joaquin County), Portola (Plumas County), Ripon (San Joaquin County), and |West Sacramento (Yolo County). Drought task forces have been established in Butte, Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought. ||Mandatory water reductions of 25 percent as ordered by Governor Brown were suspended in May due to the improved reservoir levels. Communities would set reduction guidelines based on their own projection of water supplies and the state could review the projections and impose restrictions on communities it determines were being unrealistic. In spite of the removal of the mandatory reductions, the State Water Resources Control Board announced that Californians reduced residential water use by 28 percent in May, compared with the same month in 2013.||The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted by the drought, along with California Disaster Assistance Act money for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells. Nonprofit organizations in the San Joaquin Valley struggled to meet the needs of their clients for drought-related programs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Household Water Supply Shortage Reporting System indicated there were still some homes without well water due to dry wells, with 3 in Shasta County, one more than in March. Tehama County was 34, unchanged from April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Cedar fire began on 8/16/16 5 miles north of Alta Sierra in Kern County and spread northward into Tulare County. It was human caused. The fire burned 29,322 acres mostly during August 2016, before being fully contained on 9/30/16. The fire forced evacuations for several communities in Kern and Tulare counties for more than two weeks and Highway 155 was closed for several days due to the fire. Six cabins in the Spear Creek Summer Homes tract in the community of Posey in Tulare County were destroyed. The cost of containment was $58.4 million.

Long term drought impacts continued through the month of September, although some of the largest of the reservoirs in northern interior California continued to be around normal levels. While there was a better snow pack for the mountains over the wet season, very warm weather caused the snow to melt and run off quickly in the spring. This caused peak reservoir levels to occur earlier than expected. At the end of September, all but Lake Shasta and Don Pedro were dropping off faster than normal. Lake Shasta was 105% of normal by the end of the month, Lake Oroville was 75%, Folsom Lake was 56%, and Don Pedro was 96%. New Melones continued to lag behind the other significant area reservoirs and was only 39% of normal. ||The U.S. Drought Monitor showed no change from August for the area, with severe drought across the southern Sacramento Valley; the northern and central Sacramento Valley and the northern Sierra in moderate drought, while Shasta County was abnormally dry. The remainder of the Central and northern portion of the Southern Sierra Nevada mountain areas remained in the severe to extreme drought. There remained extreme drought in the Sierra south of Highway 50. ||Groundwater aquifers continued to recharge much more slowly than the surface reservoirs, with many in the Central Valley still falling toward record levels. Growers in the San Joaquin Valley continued to turn to groundwater with reduced surface water allocations. This heavy reliance on groundwater resulted in declining water levels. ||Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect. Governor Brown issued an executive order on May 9, 2016 that built on temporary statewide emergency water restrictions to establish longer-term water conservation measures, including permanent monthly water use reporting, new permanent water use standards in California communities and banned clearly wasteful practices such as hosing off sidewalks, driveways and other hardscapes. ||Local Emergency proclamations remain for Colusa, Calaveras, El Dorado, Glenn, Plumas, San Joaquin, Shasta, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tuolumne, and Yuba counties. The cities of Live Oak (Sutter County), Lodi (San Joaquin County), Manteca (San Joaquin County), Portola (Plumas County), Ripon (San Joaquin County), and West Sacramento (Yolo County). Drought task forces remain in Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought. ||Mandatory water reductions of 25% as ordered by Governor Brown were suspended in May due to the improved reservoir levels, with voluntary reductions requested. ||The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted by the drought, along with California Disaster Assistance Act money for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells. Nonprofit organizations in the San Joaquin Valley struggled to meet the needs of their clients for drought-related programs. ||Tree mortality from drought and resulting bark beetle infestation continued to increase both in scale and intensity, especially in low elevation pine and oak woodlands along the Sierra Nevada Range. A US Forest Service- Forest Health Protection aerial survey showed tree mortality was especially high in the Stanislaus National Forest southward, though pockets of tree mortality were present as far north as eastern Shasta County. Tree mortality from the recent years of drought has been estimated at 66 million trees across California. This increase in dead and dying trees made the forest more susceptible to wildfires. ||Drought conditions are blamed for an increase in blue-green algae across Northern California lakes, with a concentration of these in Shasta County. These algae can produce cyanotoxins, which in high enough concentrations can potentially cause allergic reactions, skin irritation, fever, diarrhea, vomiting, joint pain, liver damage and even cancer in people and pets swimming in the water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Household Water Supply Shortage Reporting System indicated there were some homes without well water due to dry wells. Calaveras County had 1 household and Tuolumne County had 240 households, unchanged from August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above average temperatures and very dry conditions increased wildfire danger in early September, with the Saddle Wildfire starting on September 5th and being contained by the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 4:30 pm Monday September 5th, a total of 5 fires constituting the Saddle Fire started on Pentz Road south of Paradise, and rapidly grew in size. There were a total of 850 acres burned by containment on the 10th. The cause of the fires was determined to be a malfunctioning vehicle exhaust system. There were 3 injuries reported. There were 3 structures destroyed, 2 of those being private homes. The fire caused several power lines to go down and it affected power to customers in the area. According to PG&E, more than 300 customers were without power and significant damage was done to the power infrastructure. Evacuations were ordered for the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Enough monsoonal moisture made its way into the mountains and deserts to set off a few isolated thunderstorms, mainly in southwestern San Bernardino County. A spotter reported an area of water around 6 inches deep and about 50 feet wide flowing down Buena Vista Road near Lucerne Valley. There was also dirt and rocks on the roads, including Highway 18 and Custer Road, with a total rainfall of 1.5 inches reported. Another spotter measured one inch of rain in ten minutes elsewhere in Lucerne Valley. A flash flood warning was in effect for these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported 6 inches of water about 50 feet wide flowing down Buena Vista Road with dirt and rock on the roads, including Highway 18 and Custer Road. Total rain of 1.5 inches reported. Another spotter reported 1 inch of rain in ten minutes nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Pilot fire began on August 7 around 1106 PST near Miller Canyon OHV off of Highway 138, San Bernardino County. The fire spread rapidly to the north, with much of the total acreage burned within the first couple of days. A mandatory evacuation order was issued areas north of SR 173, south of Hesperia Lake Road, and Arrowhead Lake Road. Road closures included Highway 173 between SR 138 and Arrowhead Lake Road, as well as the Miller Canyon OHV and Forest Service Roads 2N33, 2N17X, 2N36, 2N37. Cause of the fire, as well as damaged structures and any injuries are unknown. The fire burned 8,115 acres before being fully contained on August 12 around 1200 PST.

The Goose fire began on July 30, 2016, 2 miles southeast of Prather, CA in the foothills of Fresno County. The cause is under investigation, but is believed to be human caused. It burned 1800 acres as of July 31, but continued burning into August. A portion of Highway 168 was closed for 3 days due to the fire. There were four residences and 5 out-buildings destroyed before the fire was contained on August 9, 2016. The cost of containment was $10.4 million.

The fire burned 29,322 acres and was 70 percent contained as of 8/31/16. The fire continued to burn into September. The fire forced evacuations for several communities in Kern and Tulare counties for more than two weeks and Highway 155 was closed for several days due to the fire. Six cabins in the Spear Creek Summer Homes tract in the community of Posey in Tulare County were destroyed.  The cost of containment as of 8/31 was $45 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two significant wildfires impacted sections of Santa Barbara and San Luis Obispo counties. The Chimney Fire, which ignited near Lake Nacimiento in San Luis Obispo county, burned over 46,000 acres. The Rey Fire, which ignited in the Santa Ynez Mountains in Santa Barbara county, burned over 33,000 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rey Fire ignited in southern Santa Barbara county, in the Santa Ynez Mountains. . Due to hot and dry conditions, as well as historically dry vegetation, the fire grew very quickly. The fire burned into early September, before containment and scorched 33,606 acres.

Also, communities in Central California interior face state mandated reductions in municipal water use.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent drought conditions continued to be reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A monsoon moisture push which began on July 27th continued into August 5th, bringing considerable flash flooding and isolated severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fast moving water flowed over Highway 62 in low water crossings from Steeg Road to Wonder Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Blue Cut fire began on August 16 around 0938 PST along Interstate 15 between Blue Cut & Kenwood, Swarthout Canyon/Devore, San Bernardino County. The fire spread rapidly to the north, with much of the total acreage burned within the first couple of days. A mandatory evacuation order was issued for over 84,000 people and 35,000 homes. Numerous road closures, including I��-15 in both directions for two days, with traffic building up in both directions waiting for the interstate to reopen. The Union Pacific Railroad reported burnt trusses with railroad service stopped in both directions and won't resume until trusses are rebuilt. Numerous homes and structures destroyed (approximately 105 homes and 313 minor structures). Two firefighters sustained minor injures. Two fire engines burned over as well. The fire burned 36,274 acres before being fully contained on August 22 around 0830 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mineral Fire began in 8/9/16 and was located in the foothills 9 miles southwest of Coalinga, CA. The fire burned 7050 acres before being contained on 8/18/16. The fire closed a portion of Highway 198 for a short time. There was one residence and 1 outbuilding destroyed. The cost of containment was $11.3 million.
Hot and dry conditions increased wildfire danger in mid August, with the Clayton Wildfire starting on August 13th and being contained by the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clayton Fire burned 3929 acres and destroyed 299 structures; including 189 single-family homes, 8 commercial structures, and 102 other structures such as sheds and smaller outbuildings. An additional 28 structures were damaged, though the number of structures damaged or destroyed may change as surveys are completed and additional data is confirmed. Damage to property was estimated at $1.5 million. The fire was determined to have been due to arson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought impacts continued through the month of August, although some of the largest of the reservoirs in northern interior California continued to be around normal levels. While there was a better snow pack for the mountains over the wet season, very warm weather caused the snow to melt and run off quickly in the spring. This caused peak reservoir levels to occur earlier than expected. At the end of August, all but Lake Shasta and Don Pedro were dropping off faster than normal. Lake Shasta was 105% of normal by the end of the month, Lake Oroville was 79%, Folsom Lake was 57%, and Don Pedro was 95%. New Melones continued to lag behind the other significant area reservoirs and was only 39% of normal. ||The U.S. Drought Monitor showed no change from July for the area, with severe drought across the southern Sacramento Valley; the northern and central Sacramento Valley and the northern Sierra in moderate drought, while Shasta County was abnormally dry. The remainder of the Central and northern portion of the Southern Sierra Nevada mountain areas remained in the severe to extreme drought. There remained extreme drought in the Sierra south of Highway 50. ||Groundwater aquifers continued to recharge much more slowly than the surface reservoirs, with many in the Central Valley still falling toward record levels. Growers in the San Joaquin Valley continued to turn to groundwater with reduced surface water allocations. This heavy reliance on groundwater resulted in declining water levels. ||Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect. Governor Brown issued an executive order on May 9, 2016 that built on temporary statewide emergency water restrictions to establish longer-term water conservation measures, including permanent monthly water use reporting, new permanent water use standards in California communities and banned clearly wasteful practices such as hosing off sidewalks, driveways and other hardscapes. ||Local Emergency proclamations remain for Colusa, Calaveras, El Dorado, Glenn, Plumas, San Joaquin, Shasta, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tuolumne, and Yuba counties. The cities of Live Oak (Sutter County), Lodi (San Joaquin County), Manteca (San Joaquin County), Portola (Plumas County), Ripon (San Joaquin County), and West Sacramento (Yolo County). Drought task forces remain in Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought. ||Mandatory water reductions of 25% as ordered by Governor Brown were suspended in May due to the improved reservoir levels, with voluntary reductions requested. Water conservation levels continued to fall and were around 20%, well below the 27% achieved in August 2015. ||The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted by the drought, along with California Disaster Assistance Act money for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells. Nonprofit organizations in the San Joaquin Valley struggled to meet the needs of their clients for drought-related programs. ||Tree mortality from drought and resulting bark beetle infestation continued to increase both in scale and intensity, especially in low elevation pine and oak woodlands along the Sierra Nevada Range. A US Forest Service- Forest Health Protection aerial survey showed tree mortality was especially high in the Stanislaus National Forest southward, though pockets of tree mortality were present as far north as eastern Shasta County. Tree mortality from the recent years of drought has been estimated at 66 million trees across California. This increase in dead and dying trees made the forest more susceptible to wildfires. ||Drought conditions are blamed for an increase in blue-green algae across Northern California lakes, with a concentration of these in Shasta County. These algae can produce cyanotoxins, which in high enough concentrations can potentially cause allergic reactions, skin irritation, fever, diarrhea, vomiting, joint pain, liver damage and even cancer in people and pets swimming in the water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Household Water Supply Shortage Reporting System indicated there were some homes without well water due to dry wells. Calaveras County had 1 household, unchanged from July. Tuolumne County had 240 households, an increase of 2 from July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Greenfield spin-up Classified as an EF0 Tornado||A weak non-supercell EF0 tornado occurred Friday April 22, 2016 at around 4 pm associated with a line of showers along the leading edge of a cold front. Due to its non-supercell status, it will be referred to as a landspout, but is in the family of tornados.||Friday evening, 22 April, the Monterey WFO WCM was informed by a local TV meteorologist of a video posted to facebook showing a possible tornado or dust devil. The video was posted by and on the KRKC, Greenfield radio station, facebook page. Through the course of the evening of the 22nd and morning of the 23rd, the MTR WCM and WFO staff continued investigation through following up with the reporting TV meteorologist and reaching out to KRKC via facebook and email. By Saturday morning the 23rd the address and point of contact at the storm location was obtained and a storm survey was initiated.||The WFO MTR WCM was dispatched Saturday morning to perform a storm survey. The storm survey was done through the use of the Damage Assessment Toolkit (DAT) on the office iPad. Interviews were also conducted of eyewitnesses as part of the survey.|The visual survey was done with the help of an eyewitness who described where the landspout began, the path it took, damage it inflicted, and provided the visual confirmation of rotation. According to multiple accounts, the landspout began on the hillside NW of a small farm community, laying nearly flat a broad oak tree. As the landspout moved down the hill it likely went through a period of vortex stretching, allowing it to tighten up and spin a bit faster. Eyewitnesses say the landspout moved down off the hill and toward several small houses. It displaced two normal sized steel dumpsters about 30 yards in the direction of its travel. Along this flat land portion of the path the landspout encountered two oak trees, tearing off several large branches (3in diameter) and depositing them 20-30 yards away in a downpath direction. Damage assessment indicates the landspout weakened significantly while passing over several small housing structures and brushing by a barn. Once past these structures it encountered another downhill slope and re-intensified. The landspout then encountered a pole barn with a small to moderate size trailer parked beneath. There were no visible signs of damage to the trailer, but the pole barn showed significant roof damage. It is estimated that about half of the roof from the pole barn was damaged or torn off with this debris strewn about the area. Rotation is easily confirmed due to debris locations. Parts of the roof were thrown orthogonally to the right of the direction of movement, as far as 40 to 50 yards. Other portions of the roof were lifted up, taken with the landspout and scattered across a neighboring vineyard. The farthest deposit away from the pole barn is estimated at about 60 to 80 yards, along the landspout path to the southeast. Eyewitness video posted on KRKC facebook page shows the landspout at the far edge of the vineyard and entering a rapid dissipation phase.||Eyewitness accounts also included the following:|���	No significant winds outside of the landspout|���	Ears popping as the landspout passed|���	A palette spinning in the air in the landspout���s early phase|o	No palette was found and no damage from the palette was reported|���	Thunder was heard in the area preceding the landspout|���	Brief intense heavy rain followed the landspout|���	One person reported two distinct circulations; one that descended from the cloud base and another that followed which spun up off the ground||Atmospheric and observation conditions included:|���	Occurred along the leading edge of a passing cold front|���	Cloud bases were estimated from eyewitness video to be around 5kft|���	Radar showed showers in the area|���	Lowest angle of radar beam was at about 9-10kft over the area|���	Appears to be a case of low topped convection which is normal for the immediate west coast|���	Wind data in the area did not show significant speeds.

According to multiple accounts, the landspout began on the hillside NW of a small farm community, laying nearly flat a broad oak tree. As the landspout moved down the hill it likely went through a period of vortex stretching, allowing it to tighten up and spin a bit faster. Eyewitnesses say the landspout moved down off the hill and toward several small houses. It displaced two normal sized steel dumpsters about 30 yards in the direction of its travel. Along this flat land portion of the path the landspout encountered two oak trees, tearing off several large branches (3in diameter) and depositing them 20-30 yards away in a downpath direction. Damage assessment indicates the landspout weakened significantly while passing over several small housing structures and brushing by a barn. Once past these structures it encountered another downhill slope and re-intensified. The landspout then encountered a pole barn with a small to moderate size trailer parked beneath. There were no visible signs of damage to the trailer, but the pole barn showed significant roof damage. It is estimated that about half of the roof from the pole barn was damaged or torn off with this debris strewn about the area. Rotation is easily confirmed due to debris locations. Parts of the roof were thrown orthogonally to the right of the direction of movement, as far as 40 to 50 yards. Other portions of the roof were lifted up, taken with the landspout and scattered across a neighboring vineyard. The farthest deposit away from the pole barn is estimated at about 60 to 80 yards, along the landspout path to the southeast. Eyewitness video posted on KRKC facebook page shows the landspout at the far edge of the vineyard and entering a rapid dissipation phase.||Eyewitness accounts also included the following:.|No significant winds outside of the landspout.|Ears popping as the landspout passed.|A palette spinning in the air in the landspout���s early phase.|No palette was found and no damage from the palette was reported.|Thunder was heard in the area preceding the landspout.|Brief intense heavy rain followed the landspout.|One person reported two distinct circulations; one that descended from the cloud base and another that followed which spun up off the ground.||Atmospheric and observation conditions included:.|Occurred along the leading edge of a passing cold front.|Cloud bases were estimated from eyewitness video to be around 5kft.|Radar showed showers in the area.|Lowest angle of radar beam was at about 9-10kft over the area.|Appears to be a case of low topped convection which is normal for the immediate west coast.|Wind data in the area did not show significant speeds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continues during the month of August, 2016. Both Fresno and Bakersfield received no precipitation during the month. No precipitation and slightly above normal temperatures we received this month did allow for increased evaporation rates and soil moisture to decrease. ||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The general trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures has continued this year. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Also, communities in Central California interior face state mandated reductions in municipal water use.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent drought conditions continued to be reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high winds to several locations around northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bolam RAWS recorded many gusts exceeding 58 mph between 13/1603 PST and 13/2303 PST.  The peak gust was 66 mph recorded at 13/2303 PST. The Indian Well RAWS recorded a gust to 61 mph at 13/2301 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high winds to several locations around northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Timber Creek RAWS recorded gusts exceeding 61 mph continuously between 13/2104 PST and 14/0103 PST.  The peak gust was 73 mph recorded at 13/2303 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief long period south swell brought high surf to the beaches of Orange County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lifeguards at Huntington beach reported 6 to 8 ft surf with occasional sets to 9 ft on the 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second cold front brought even stronger winds to Northwest California only a couple days after the first strong storm system of the season. Winds exceeded 60 mph in Crescent City with several reports of damage. Additional strong wind gusts were recorded in the mountains. Nearly a foot of rain fell over the 72 hour period from the two storms with minor flooding reported in Del Norte County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many gusts between 50 and 67 mph around midday. Numerous trees fell across the coastal area of Del Norte County resulting in widespread power outages and closed roads including Highway 101.

At least one tree fell across Highway 199 blocking the road just east of Jedediah Smith Redwoods State Park.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 178 was closed from the Nevada state line to Shoshone, and Highway 127 was closed from Shoshone to Death Valley Junction.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 190 was flooded with water and mud at mile markers 97-100.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Erskine Fire began on June 23, 2016 along Erskine Creek Road, 1 mile southwest of Lake Isabella in Kern County. The fire spread rapidly due to strong, gusty winds, hot temperatures and dry conditions on the June 23 through 25. The growth of the fire slowed as winds decreased by June 26, however the fire continued to burn through early July before being contained on July 9. It burned 48,019 acres. The fire closed Highway 178 east of Bakersfield for several days with evacuation of numerous small communities in the Lake Isabella area. The fire destroyed 285 residences and 1 commercial building as well as damaging 12 other residences. There were 2 fatalities on June 23, 2016 as an elderly couple were unable to escape the rapidly moving fire. See June Storm Data for details. The cost of containment was $23 million.

The Goose fire began on July 30, 2016, 2 miles southeast of Prather, CA in the foothills of Fresno County. The cause is under investigation, but is believed to be human caused. It burned 1800 acres as of July 31, but continued burning into August. A portion of Highway 168 was closed for 3 days due to the fire. The cost of containment was $1.2 Million as of July 31.

Westerly flow with an upper level jet ahead of a nearly stationary west to east orientated cold front over central California brought strong winds to the higher elevations of the Kern County Mountains and allowed gusty winds to develop over parts of the Kern County Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report received from Cache Creek NR6G-6 (AU563) APRS station of 28-31 mph sustained winds between 10/17 0658 PST and 10/17 0705 PST with a peak wind gust of 59 mph at 0658 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe weather and flash flooding which began on June 27th with the first monsoon moisture push of the season continued through July 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down a power pole and power lines 11 miles ESE of Furnace Creek, knocking out power to portions of Death Valley for 38 hours. Winds also damaged several roofs in the ranger housing area and flipped a small plane parked in Saline Valley.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight to 12 inches of water flowed over Rimrock Road near and east of H Street. A swift water rescue occurred at Center St. and Upton Dr. Water, mud, and debris overflowed the Guadalupe Channel, causing significant damage to two homes on Creosote Ct. Flooding and debris covered all lanes of Highway 247 at mile marker 77.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of very dry vegetation and hot and dry weather continued to lead to several wildfires across Southern California. Some fires that started in June (Sherpa, Pine and San Gabriel Complex) continued to burn into June. Additionally, two more significant fires (Sand and Sage fires) developed during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sherpa Fire, which started in mid June, was finally contained in early July. In total, the fire burned 7,474 acres in the mountains of Santa Barbara county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of heat and low humidity increased wildfire danger at the beginning of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Trailhead Fire started on June 28th and continued until containment on July 18th. The cause is still under investigation. There were a total of 5646 acres burned, with a total of 3 firefighters injured. The were 2600 structures threatened, with extensive evacuations in the area around Todd Valley and Forest Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought impacts continued through the month of July, although some of the largest of the reservoirs in northern interior California were around normal levels. While there was a better snow pack for the mountains over the wet season, very warm weather caused the snow to melt and run off quickly in the spring. This caused peak reservoir levels to occur earlier than expected. By the end of July, all but Lake Shasta and Don Pedro were dropping off faster than normal. Lake Shasta was 110% of normal by the end of the month, Lake Oroville was 90%, Folsom Lake was 70%, and Don Pedro was 94%. New Melones continued to lag behind the other significant area reservoirs and was only 40% of normal.||The U.S. Drought Monitor showed no change from June for the area, with severe drought across the southern Sacramento Valley; the northern and central Sacramento Valley and the northern Sierra in moderate drought, while Shasta County was abnormally dry. The remainder of the Central and northern portion of the Southern Sierra Nevada mountain areas remained in the severe to extreme drought. There remained extreme drought in the Sierra south of Highway 50. ||Groundwater aquifers continued to recharge much more slowly than the surface reservoirs, with many in the Central Valley still falling toward record levels. Growers in the San Joaquin Valley continued to turn to groundwater with reduced surface water allocations. This heavy reliance on groundwater resulted in declining water levels. ||Governor Jerry Brown declared a drought emergency for the entire state of California January 17, 2014 and this continued to be in effect. Governor Brown issued an executive order on May 9, 2016 that built on temporary statewide emergency water restrictions to establish longer-term water conservation measures, including permanent monthly water use reporting, new permanent water use standards in California communities and banned clearly wasteful practices such as hosing off sidewalks, driveways and other hardscapes. ||Local Emergency proclamations remained for Colusa, Calaveras, El Dorado, Glenn, Plumas, San Joaquin, Shasta, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tuolumne, and Yuba counties. The cities of Live Oak (Sutter County), Lodi (San Joaquin County), Manteca (San Joaquin County), Portola (Plumas County), Ripon (San Joaquin County), and West Sacramento (Yolo County). Drought task forces remain in Lake, Nevada, Placer, Plumas, Sacramento, San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Sutter, Tehama, Tuolumne, and Yolo counties to coordinate response to the drought. ||Mandatory water reductions of 25% as ordered by Governor Brown were suspended in May due to the improved reservoir levels, with voluntary reductions requested. Water conservation was reported as about the same as the 21.5% savings for the month of June 2016, although it was down from 31% in July 2015. ||The state continued to supply food assistance to those impacted by the drought, along with California Disaster Assistance Act money for those who have lost drinking water due to dry wells. Nonprofit organizations in the San Joaquin Valley struggled to meet the needs of their clients for drought-related programs. ||Tree mortality from drought and resulting bark beetle infestation continued to increase both in scale and intensity, especially in low elevation pine and oak woodlands along the Sierra Nevada Range. A US Forest Service- Forest Health Protection aerial survey showed tree mortality was especially high in the Stanislaus National Forest southward, though pockets of tree mortality were present as far north as eastern Shasta County. Tree mortality from the recent years of drought has been estimated at 66 million trees across California. This increase in dead and dying trees made the forest more susceptible to wildfires.||Drought conditions are blamed for an increase in blue-green algae across Northern California lakes. These algae can produce cyanotoxins, which in high enough concentrations can potentially cause allergic reactions, skin irritation, fever, diarrhea, vomiting, joint pain, liver damage and even cancer in people and pets swimming in the water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Household Water Supply Shortage Reporting System indicated there were some homes without well water due to dry wells. Calaveras County had 1 household and Tuolumne County had 240 households, unchanged from June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure over the southwestern continental United States brought triple digit heat to the central California region including the San Joaquin Valley and Kern County desert areas. Excessive heat was observed in parts of the Kern County desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mojave ASOS (KMHV) reported a high temperature of 106 degrees on 7/30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The California drought continues during the month of July, 2016. Both Fresno and Bakersfield received no precipitation during the month. No precipitation and above normal temperatures we received this month did allow for increased evaporation rates and soil moisture to decrease. ||The U.S. Drought Monitor continued to report exceptional drought conditions across the entire Central California region. This extent of exceptional drought is extremely unusual for California. The general trend of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures has continued this year. ||There continues to be significant media coverage on the on-going drought conditions. These reports include discussion of significant re-allocation of water resources from the east to west side of the San Joaquin Valley, farmers forgoing planting of some crops, loss or reduction of ground water, wells drying up in several communities leaving them with no water, and an unprecedented increase in fire danger across the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Also, communities in Central California interior face state mandated reductions in municipal water use.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent drought conditions continued to be reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly flow with an upper level jet ahead of a nearly stationary west to east orientated cold front over central California brought strong winds to the higher elevations of the Kern County Mountains and allowed gusty winds to develop over parts of the Kern County Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report received from Jawbone Canyon (JWBC1) RAWS station of 42-53 mph sustained winds between 10/17 0314 PST and 10/17 1014 PST with a peak wind gust of 69 mph at 0414 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another push of monsoon moisture led to isolated severe weather and flash flooding over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. The episode continued through August 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two feet of water covered Hobbs Circle. Water in the storm drain blew off a manhole cover and spouted like a geyser.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure system and associated frontal boundary from the Pacific Northwest moved southeast through central California region increasing winds across the area and especially in parts of the Kern County desert areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cache Creek APRS (AU563) reported sustained wind speeds 35 to 42 mph with wind gusts of 49 to 60 mph between 1730 PST and 2329 PST on 7/10. A maximum wind gust of 60 mph was reported at 1754 PST on 7/10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Soup Complex wildfire started on 09/17/16 at 1619 PDT.  The cause remains unknown.  As of 0430 PDT on 10/07/16 , the fire covered 2722 acres and was 70% contained.  No structures were lost and 5.4 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Soup Complex wildfire started on 09/17/16 at 1619 PDT.  The cause remains unknown.  As of 0430 PDT on 10/07/16 , the fire covered 2722 acres and was 70% contained.  No structures were lost and 5.4 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two low pressure systems brought a combination of strong winds and significant rain and high elevation snow on the 14th and 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of steep terrain (cliff face) and significant rain caused a large rockfall onto Tioga Pass Road (CA 120), causing the shutdown of the road. The time of the rockfall is estimated and based around the time of the heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow southeasterly moving low pressure system coupled with northward moving tropical moisture brought heavy valley and foothill rains, heavy mountain snows, and gusty winds to parts of the central California interior. Precipitation was at times enhanced due to orographical lifting ahead of the slow southerly moving cold front associated with the low pressure system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Devils Postpile snotel reported an estimated storm total snowfall of 13.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moved through the San Francisco Bay Area and Monterey Bay Area on Thursday, December 15, 2016. The front moved through the area slowly, producing moderate to locally heavy rain for several hours which resulted in nuerous reports of flooding, especially for the Northern San Francisco Bay Area.  Also, this storm system produced locally strong winds with gusts greater than 50 mph. These winds brough down drought-stressed trees which damaged power lines and cares.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northbound SR 121 closed due to flooding - Junction of Route 116 and 121, Arnold Drive, Fremont Drive to Schellville Road, Eighth Street.
